questions.text: Which of the following BEST describes a public cloud deployment model?

answers.text: Cloud infrastructure owned and operated exclusively by a single organization for their private use
Cloud infrastructure owned and operated by a third-party provider, shared among multiple customers over the internet, with resources provisioned on demand
Cloud infrastructure shared only among organizations within the same industry sector
Cloud infrastructure hosted in an organization's own data center managed by a third party

explanations.a: Infrastructure exclusively for one organization describes a private cloud — not public cloud.
explanations.b: Public cloud is infrastructure owned and operated by providers like AWS, Azure, or GCP — shared among multiple customers (multi-tenancy) and accessed over the internet with resources available on demand. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Infrastructure shared within an industry sector describes a community cloud — not a public cloud.
explanations.d: Organization-owned infrastructure managed by a third party describes a managed private cloud — not a public cloud.

questions.text: A startup needs to launch a web application quickly with no upfront infrastructure investment, paying only for what they use. Which cloud deployment model BEST meets this need?

answers.text: Private cloud requiring significant upfront capital investment
Public cloud offering immediate access to infrastructure with pay-as-you-go billing and no upfront hardware costs
Community cloud restricted to specific industry members
On-premises infrastructure purchased and installed in the startup's office

explanations.a: Private cloud requires significant capital expenditure for hardware and infrastructure — the opposite of what a startup with no upfront investment budget needs.
explanations.b: Public cloud provides immediate infrastructure access with no hardware purchase, pay-as-you-go billing, and unlimited elasticity — ideal for startups needing fast time-to-market with minimal capital outlay. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Community clouds serve specific industry groups — a startup would not typically have access or meet the membership requirements.
explanations.d: Purchasing and installing on-premises hardware requires significant upfront investment and time — not suitable for a quick launch.

questions.text: A company evaluating public cloud deployment raises concerns about data sovereignty — specifically that their customer data must remain within their country's borders. How does this concern apply to public cloud?

answers.text: Public cloud providers automatically keep all customer data within the customer's country regardless of configuration

Public cloud data sovereignty requires careful region selection and configuration — data may be replicated globally by default, so customers must explicitly configure data residency controls and select compliant regions to meet legal requirements
Public cloud providers are legally exempt from data sovereignty regulations
Data sovereignty concerns only apply to private cloud deployments

explanations.a: Public cloud does not automatically enforce data residency — customers must explicitly configure region selection and data replication settings.
explanations.b: Public cloud data sovereignty compliance requires deliberately selecting regions within the required jurisdiction, disabling cross-region replication that could move data outside legal boundaries, and configuring any global services (CDNs, backups) to enforce residency constraints. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Public cloud providers are absolutely subject to data sovereignty regulations — compliance is a shared responsibility.
explanations.d: Data sovereignty is equally applicable — and often more challenging — in public cloud due to global infrastructure than in private cloud where location is controlled.

questions.text: Which of the following is a KEY trade-off of the public cloud model compared to private cloud?

answers.text: Public cloud offers more customization and control over the underlying infrastructure than private cloud

Public cloud provides greater elasticity and lower capital expenditure at the cost of less control over the underlying infrastructure, shared tenancy security considerations, and potential data residency limitations
Public cloud always has lower latency than private cloud for all workloads
Public cloud eliminates all security responsibilities from the customer

explanations.a: Private cloud offers more customization and control — public cloud trades control for convenience and elasticity.
explanations.b: Public cloud's elasticity and OpEx model come with trade-offs: customers share physical infrastructure with others (multi-tenancy), have limited control over the underlying hardware, and must carefully manage data residency — all addressed by the shared responsibility model. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Latency depends on network topology and proximity — private cloud co-located with an application may have lower latency than a distant public cloud region.
explanations.d: The shared responsibility model clearly defines customer security responsibilities in public cloud — providers manage physical security but customers manage data, identity, and application security.

questions.text: What distinguishes a private cloud from a public cloud?

answers.text: A private cloud is operated by a public cloud provider for exclusive use by a single customer
A private cloud is cloud infrastructure dedicated exclusively to a single organization — not shared with other tenants — providing greater control, customization, and isolation
A private cloud requires internet connectivity for all resource access
A private cloud charges customers per hour of resource usage like public cloud

explanations.a: A cloud operated by a public provider for exclusive use describes a dedicated or hosted private cloud — but the defining characteristic is single-tenant exclusivity, not who operates it.
explanations.b: Private cloud is defined by its single-tenant, dedicated nature — all infrastructure resources are reserved for and controlled by one organization, providing complete isolation from other customers. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Private clouds can be accessed without internet — internal private clouds use private corporate network connectivity.
explanations.d: Private cloud billing models vary — organizations may use chargeback or showback internally, but it is not billed hourly by an external provider.

questions.text: A government agency requires complete control over their infrastructure, strict data isolation from other organizations, and the ability to run classified workloads. Which cloud deployment model is MOST appropriate?

answers.text: Public cloud with encryption enabled on all storage
Private cloud providing dedicated infrastructure with complete isolation and full organizational control
Community cloud shared with other government agencies
Hybrid cloud using a mix of public and private resources

explanations.a: Public cloud with encryption still operates in a shared multi-tenant environment — insufficient for classified workloads requiring complete physical isolation.
explanations.b: Private cloud provides the complete infrastructure dedication, physical isolation from other organizations, and full organizational control required for classified government workloads. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Community cloud shared with other agencies still involves multi-tenant infrastructure — classified workloads require exclusive dedicated infrastructure.
explanations.d: Hybrid cloud exposes some workloads to public infrastructure — classified workloads should not run on shared public infrastructure.

questions.text: A company operates a private cloud using VMware on-premises. They want to extend it with cloud-like self-service provisioning, automated scaling, and a service catalog for internal teams. Which approach modernizes the private cloud without migrating to public cloud?

answers.text: Migrate all workloads to AWS immediately
Deploy a private cloud management platform such as OpenStack or VMware vRealize Automation to add self-service portals, automated provisioning, and orchestration on top of the existing infrastructure
Purchase additional physical servers to increase capacity
Convert all VMs to containers and deploy them to Docker Swarm

explanations.a: Migrating to AWS converts to public cloud — the scenario specifically requires retaining private cloud.
explanations.b: Private cloud management platforms add cloud-like capabilities (self-service portals, automated provisioning, service catalogs, chargeback) on top of existing on-premises infrastructure — modernizing the private cloud experience without migrating to public cloud. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Adding servers increases raw capacity but does not add cloud-like self-service or automation capabilities.
explanations.d: Converting to containers changes the workload model but does not add self-service provisioning, scaling automation, or a service catalog to the private cloud.

questions.text: Which of the following is the PRIMARY disadvantage of private cloud compared to public cloud?

answers.text: Private cloud cannot run virtualized workloads

Private cloud requires significant upfront capital investment for hardware, ongoing maintenance costs, and dedicated staff — with limited elasticity compared to public cloud's virtually unlimited on-demand capacity
Private cloud provides less security isolation than public cloud
Private cloud cannot be used for production workloads

explanations.a: Private clouds are built on hypervisors and run virtualized workloads — this is a core capability, not a limitation.
explanations.b: The primary disadvantages of private cloud are: significant CapEx for hardware procurement, ongoing OpEx for maintenance and staffing, and limited elasticity constrained by physical hardware capacity — unlike public cloud which provides virtually unlimited on-demand resources with no upfront investment. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Private cloud typically provides greater security isolation than public cloud — single tenancy is one of its key advantages.
explanations.d: Private clouds are extensively used for production workloads — this statement is incorrect.

questions.text: What does on-premises infrastructure refer to in a cloud context?

answers.text: Computing resources hosted in a public cloud provider's data center
Computing infrastructure physically located and operated within an organization's own facilities — managed and maintained by the organization's own IT staff
Cloud resources reserved exclusively for a single organization
Infrastructure managed by a cloud provider on behalf of a customer

explanations.a: Resources in a public cloud provider's data center are cloud-hosted — not on-premises.
explanations.b: On-premises (on-prem) refers to computing infrastructure physically located in the organization's own data center or office facilities — purchased, owned, and operated by the organization's IT team. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Reserved cloud resources exclusively for one organization describes a private or dedicated cloud — not on-premises.
explanations.d: Infrastructure managed by a cloud provider describes a managed service or hosted model — not on-premises.

questions.text: A financial institution keeps their core banking systems on-premises despite pressure to migrate to cloud. Which factor MOST commonly justifies retaining on-premises infrastructure for such systems?

answers.text: On-premises hardware is always faster than cloud infrastructure

Regulatory compliance requirements, data sovereignty mandates, existing large capital investments, and latency-sensitive integration with other on-premises systems make cloud migration complex or non-compliant
Cloud providers do not offer financial services compliant infrastructure
On-premises systems cannot be integrated with cloud services

explanations.a: Cloud infrastructure can match or exceed on-premises performance — raw speed is not the primary retention justification.
explanations.b: Financial institutions face strict regulatory requirements about where data resides and how it is processed, significant existing capital investments in on-premises systems, and complex low-latency dependencies between systems that make wholesale migration challenging without impacting operations or compliance. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Major cloud providers offer FedRAMP, PCI DSS, SOC2, and other financial compliance certifications — this statement is incorrect.
explanations.d: On-premises systems can be integrated with cloud services through VPNs, dedicated connections, and hybrid architectures — this statement is incorrect.

questions.text: An organization runs latency-sensitive manufacturing control systems that must respond in under 5ms. They want cloud-like management capabilities but cannot tolerate the network latency of a public cloud. Which approach BEST addresses both requirements?

answers.text: Migrate all control systems to the nearest public cloud region
Deploy cloud-managed on-premises infrastructure such as AWS Outposts or Azure Stack — bringing cloud services and APIs to on-premises hardware while keeping processing local for sub-5ms response
Accept the public cloud latency and increase system timeout thresholds
Disconnect the control systems from all network connectivity

explanations.a: Migrating to public cloud introduces network latency that cannot achieve sub-5ms response for manufacturing control systems — this violates the core requirement.
explanations.b: Cloud-managed on-premises solutions like AWS Outposts or Azure Stack bring cloud APIs, management planes, and services to on-premises hardware — enabling cloud-like management while keeping actual processing local with sub-millisecond physical proximity to the controlled equipment. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Increasing timeout thresholds on a sub-5ms system would break real-time control functionality — not a viable compromise.
explanations.d: Disconnecting from network eliminates management capabilities entirely — the opposite of adding cloud-like management.

questions.text: A company's on-premises data center lease expires in 18 months. Leadership wants to evaluate total cost of ownership (TCO) for on-premises renewal versus cloud migration. Which cost factors are MOST important to include in the on-premises TCO analysis?

answers.text: Only the cost of new server hardware

Hardware procurement, data center lease/construction, power and cooling, network infrastructure, software licensing, IT staff salaries for operations and maintenance, and hardware refresh cycles over a 5-year horizon
Only the monthly electricity bill for the data center
Only the cost of cloud migration professional services

explanations.a: Hardware is one component — a complete TCO analysis must include all direct and indirect costs over the evaluation period.
explanations.b: A complete on-premises TCO analysis must capture all cost dimensions: capital hardware, facility costs (lease, power, cooling, physical security), network infrastructure, software licensing, and crucially the full labor cost of IT staff maintaining the environment over a multi-year horizon. Missing any of these understates true on-premises cost. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Electricity is one operational expense — a TCO analysis covering only electricity would vastly understate actual on-premises cost.
explanations.d: Migration professional services are a cloud transition cost — not an on-premises TCO component.

questions.text: What is a hybrid cloud deployment model?

answers.text: A cloud environment shared among multiple competing organizations
A combination of private cloud or on-premises infrastructure with public cloud services, connected to enable data and application portability between environments
A single public cloud environment using multiple availability zones
A cloud model where all resources are managed by a community of users

explanations.a: Multiple competing organizations sharing cloud describes community or public cloud — not hybrid.
explanations.b: Hybrid cloud combines private/on-premises infrastructure with public cloud resources, connected through dedicated links or VPNs — allowing workloads to move between environments based on policy, capacity, or cost requirements. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Multiple availability zones within a single public cloud is a redundancy strategy within one cloud model — not hybrid cloud.
explanations.d: Community-managed resources describe a community cloud — not hybrid cloud.

questions.text: A retailer keeps customer payment data on their private on-premises servers for compliance but uses public cloud for their customer-facing e-commerce website and analytics. This architecture is an example of which deployment model?

answers.text: Public cloud
Private cloud
Hybrid cloud
Community cloud

explanations.a: Pure public cloud would host all workloads including payment data on the provider's infrastructure — not the described architecture.
explanations.b: Pure private cloud would host all workloads on dedicated private infrastructure — not the described architecture using public cloud for the website.
explanations.c: The combination of on-premises private infrastructure for compliance-sensitive payment data with public cloud for the e-commerce website and analytics is the defining example of hybrid cloud deployment. This is the correct answer.
explanations.d: Community cloud is shared among organizations in the same sector — not a combination of private and public environments.

questions.text: A hybrid cloud architect must ensure consistent security policies, network connectivity, and identity management across both on-premises and public cloud environments. Which challenge is MOST significant in maintaining hybrid cloud consistency?

answers.text: Ensuring both environments use the same hardware vendor

Maintaining consistent identity federation, network security policies, firewall rules, and compliance controls across two fundamentally different environments with different management tools, APIs, and security models
Ensuring both environments have the same physical rack layout
Making sure both environments run the same hypervisor version

explanations.a: Hardware vendor consistency is irrelevant in a hybrid cloud — virtualization abstracts the underlying hardware.
explanations.b: Hybrid cloud's primary management challenge is policy consistency across environments that use different tooling, APIs, and security models. Identity federation (SSO across both), consistent firewall rules, unified compliance monitoring, and coherent network security require significant integration effort. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Physical rack layout is a data center operations detail — irrelevant to hybrid cloud management consistency.
explanations.d: Hypervisor versions are infrastructure management details — not the primary consistency challenge in hybrid cloud environments.

questions.text: An organization uses hybrid cloud with cloud bursting — running baseline workloads on-premises and bursting to public cloud during peak demand. A security audit finds that data moving between environments is not encrypted in transit. What is the MOST critical remediation?

answers.text: Disable cloud bursting to prevent data from leaving the on-premises environment
Implement TLS encryption for all data in transit between on-premises and public cloud, and use a VPN or dedicated connection with encryption to protect data traversing the hybrid network boundary
Move all workloads permanently to public cloud to eliminate the transit boundary
Encrypt only the databases on-premises and leave transit traffic unencrypted

explanations.a: Disabling cloud bursting eliminates the business benefit of hybrid cloud — not a proportionate security response.
explanations.b: Encrypting data in transit across hybrid cloud boundaries using TLS for application-layer data and VPN/dedicated connection encryption for the network path protects data from interception while preserving the hybrid cloud bursting capability. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Moving all workloads to public cloud changes the entire architectural model — not a targeted remediation for in-transit encryption.
explanations.d: Encrypting only on-premises databases while leaving transit traffic unencrypted provides no protection against interception of data as it moves between environments.

questions.text: What is a community cloud deployment model?

answers.text: A cloud environment accessible to anyone on the internet
A cloud infrastructure shared among a specific group of organizations with common interests, requirements, or compliance obligations — such as government agencies or healthcare providers
A cloud environment operated by a single organization for their exclusive use
A combination of public and private cloud resources connected by a network

explanations.a: A cloud accessible to anyone on the internet describes public cloud — not community cloud.
explanations.b: Community cloud is a shared model for a defined group of organizations with common concerns — such as the same regulatory requirements, security classification level, or industry standards. Members share infrastructure costs while maintaining more isolation than public cloud. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: A single organization's exclusive cloud describes private cloud — not community cloud.
explanations.d: A combination of public and private resources describes hybrid cloud — not community cloud.

questions.text: Several hospitals in the same region need to share a cloud platform for patient data exchange, medical imaging storage, and clinical research collaboration. All must comply with HIPAA. Which cloud model BEST serves this group?

answers.text: Public cloud with each hospital using a separate account
Community cloud shared among the hospitals — purpose-built for healthcare compliance, enabling collaboration while maintaining appropriate data isolation between institutions
Each hospital building its own private cloud independently
Hybrid cloud with patient data in public cloud and imaging on-premises

explanations.a: Separate public cloud accounts would enable collaboration only through complex cross-account setups — not designed for the shared compliance and collaboration needs of the hospital group.
explanations.b: Community cloud is specifically designed for this scenario — a shared platform built around common compliance requirements (HIPAA), enabling collaboration between member institutions while maintaining appropriate access controls and data isolation between hospitals. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Each hospital building its own private cloud prevents the collaborative data sharing and research the scenario requires.
explanations.d: Hybrid cloud mixes public and private environments — but placing patient data in public cloud raises HIPAA compliance concerns without the purpose-built compliance controls of a community health cloud.

questions.text: A community cloud is shared among five financial institutions with the same regulatory requirements. One institution's workload experiences a security incident. Which community cloud characteristic presents the GREATEST risk to the other member institutions?

answers.text: The community cloud uses shared storage which costs more than private cloud

Shared underlying infrastructure means a security incident in one member's environment could potentially affect shared platform components — requiring robust logical isolation, network segmentation, and incident response procedures that protect all members
Community cloud members cannot communicate with each other's systems
Community cloud does not support multi-factor authentication

explanations.a: Cost sharing in community cloud typically reduces per-member cost — shared storage is not a security risk.
explanations.b: Community cloud members share infrastructure — a security compromise in one member's environment could potentially reach shared platform components (network segments, storage, management planes) if logical isolation is insufficient. This lateral movement risk must be mitigated through strong network segmentation and isolation controls. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Community clouds specifically enable controlled communication between members for collaboration — this statement is incorrect.
explanations.d: Community clouds can and should require MFA — this is not an inherent limitation.

questions.text: Which of the following BEST differentiates community cloud from both public and private cloud?

answers.text: Community cloud is always cheaper than both public and private cloud

Community cloud is shared like public cloud but restricted to a defined group of organizations with common requirements — offering more customization and compliance alignment than public cloud while distributing costs across members unlike private cloud
Community cloud requires all member organizations to be located in the same building
Community cloud can only be used by government organizations

explanations.a: Community cloud is not universally cheaper — cost depends on member count, compliance requirements, and infrastructure design.
explanations.b: Community cloud occupies a unique position: it shares infrastructure costs across member organizations (unlike private cloud) but restricts access to a defined community with shared requirements (unlike open public cloud) — enabling purpose-built compliance, governance, and collaboration features tailored to the specific community's needs. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Community cloud members can be geographically distributed — co-location is not required.
explanations.d: Community clouds serve many sectors — healthcare, finance, defense, research — not exclusively government.

questions.text: What is the defining characteristic of a blue-green deployment strategy?

answers.text: Gradually replacing old instances with new ones one at a time
Maintaining two identical production environments — one running the current version (blue) and one running the new version (green) — with traffic switched between them during deployment
Sending a small percentage of traffic to the new version before full rollout
Updating all instances simultaneously with no overlap between versions

explanations.a: Replacing instances one at a time describes a rolling deployment — not blue-green.
explanations.b: Blue-green deployment maintains two parallel environments. The blue environment serves live traffic while the green environment runs the new version. Traffic is switched all at once (or gradually) to green, and blue becomes the instant rollback target if issues arise. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Sending a small percentage of traffic to the new version describes a canary deployment — not blue-green.
explanations.d: Updating all instances simultaneously with no overlap describes an in-place deployment — not blue-green.

questions.text: A team deploys a new application version using blue-green deployment. After switching traffic to the green environment, users report a critical bug. What is the PRIMARY advantage of blue-green in this scenario?

answers.text: The team can immediately roll back by switching traffic back to the blue environment with no downtime
The bug is automatically fixed by the blue-green system
The green environment is automatically deleted to prevent further issues
The team must redeploy the previous version to all instances before traffic can be restored

explanations.a: With the blue environment still intact and running the previous version, rollback is simply redirecting traffic back to blue — taking seconds with no downtime or redeployment required. This is the correct answer.
explanations.b: Blue-green deployment does not include automated bug detection or correction capabilities.
explanations.c: The green environment is kept available as the rollback option — it is not automatically deleted on failure.
explanations.d: Redeploying the previous version is what other deployment strategies require — blue-green's entire advantage is that the previous version remains live and instantly switchable.

questions.text: A blue-green deployment switches all traffic from the blue to the green environment. The new version makes breaking schema changes to the shared database. The team needs to roll back to blue. What challenge does this present?

answers.text: Traffic cannot be switched back to blue because DNS TTL prevents rapid changes

The blue environment uses the same database — the breaking schema changes made by green may have corrupted data structures that the blue version cannot read, making rollback complex or impossible without a database restore
The blue environment was automatically deleted when green went live
Blue-green deployments do not support database applications

explanations.a: DNS TTL can be pre-set low before deployment to enable rapid switches — this is a known blue-green preparation step, not a fundamental blocker.
explanations.b: Database schema changes are the primary complication for blue-green rollbacks. If the green version applies breaking schema migrations (dropping columns, changing data types), the blue version — which expects the old schema — cannot function correctly with the modified database without a database restore or backward-compatible migration strategy. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: In a correctly implemented blue-green deployment, the blue environment is preserved for rollback — it is not deleted automatically.
explanations.d: Blue-green deployments absolutely support database applications — schema management is a complexity to plan for, not an incompatibility.

questions.text: Which of the following is the PRIMARY cost consideration of blue-green deployments compared to other strategies?

answers.text: Blue-green deployments require expensive dedicated hardware unavailable in cloud environments
Blue-green requires running two complete production environments simultaneously during deployment — effectively doubling infrastructure costs during the transition period
Blue-green deployments are always the cheapest strategy because they avoid downtime costs
Blue-green has no additional cost because cloud environments are provisioned at no charge

explanations.a: Blue-green deployments work perfectly in cloud environments — cloud elasticity actually makes them easier to implement.
explanations.b: The fundamental cost of blue-green is maintaining two full production environments simultaneously during deployment. While the transition may be brief, organizations must plan for the cost of running double infrastructure — even temporarily. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Avoiding downtime has business value, but blue-green is not the cheapest infrastructure strategy — it requires double the resources during deployment.
explanations.d: Cloud resources always have a cost — running two identical production environments incurs double the infrastructure charges during the transition.

questions.text: What is a canary deployment strategy?

answers.text: Deploying the new version to all instances simultaneously
Gradually routing a small percentage of production traffic to the new version while the majority continues to the old version — monitoring for issues before expanding the rollout
Running two complete identical environments and switching all traffic at once
Replacing instances one by one with the new version until all are updated

explanations.a: Deploying to all instances simultaneously describes an in-place deployment — not canary.
explanations.b: Canary deployment routes a small initial percentage of traffic (e.g., 5%) to the new version. If monitoring shows no issues, traffic is progressively increased until full rollout — limiting the blast radius of any defects to a small subset of users. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Two complete identical environments with all-at-once traffic switching describes blue-green — not canary.
explanations.d: Replacing instances one by one describes a rolling deployment — not canary.

questions.text: A team uses canary deployment and routes 10% of traffic to the new version. Monitoring shows elevated error rates on the canary instances. What action should the team take?

answers.text: Immediately route 100% of traffic to the new version to expose the issue to more users for faster debugging
Roll back by redirecting all traffic back to the stable version — limiting the impact to the 10% who experienced the issue while the team investigates
Increase traffic to 50% to gather more data before deciding
Ignore the elevated error rate and wait for users to report specific issues

explanations.a: Routing 100% of traffic to a version with elevated errors would expose all users to the defect — the opposite of what canary deployment is designed to prevent.
explanations.b: Detecting issues on the canary and rolling back traffic to the stable version is the exact purpose of canary deployment — limiting user impact to the small percentage that was routed to the new version while the team investigates safely. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Increasing traffic to a version showing elevated errors expands the blast radius — directly contradicting the canary strategy's risk-limiting goal.
explanations.d: Ignoring monitoring signals defeats the entire purpose of canary deployment — proactive detection and rollback before full rollout is the strategy's core value.

questions.text: A team implements canary deployment for a new API version. They route 5% of traffic to the new version and monitor for 24 hours with no issues detected. They then route 25%, then 50%, then 100% over the following days. What does this progressive traffic shifting strategy accomplish?

answers.text: It ensures equal distribution of load across all server instances

It systematically validates the new version under progressively larger real-world load conditions — building confidence at each stage before expanding exposure and minimizing risk if a defect emerges at higher traffic volumes
It allows the team to charge different prices to canary users
It automatically performs A/B testing to measure feature adoption rates

explanations.a: Load distribution is a load balancing concern — progressive canary shifting is about risk management, not equal load distribution.
explanations.b: Progressive traffic shifting validates the new version under increasing real-world load — issues that only manifest under higher load (race conditions, database connection pool exhaustion, memory leaks) are caught at limited exposure before full rollout, with rollback still fast and low-impact at each stage. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Canary deployment is an operational strategy — it has no mechanism or purpose for differential pricing.
explanations.d: A/B testing measures user behavior differences between versions — canary deployment measures operational stability, not feature adoption metrics.

questions.text: Which scenario would make canary deployment LESS effective as a risk mitigation strategy?

answers.text: The application has comprehensive monitoring and alerting configured
The new version introduces a database migration that runs on first startup and affects all users' data — regardless of which traffic percentage they are routed to
The team has automated rollback capabilities configured
Traffic routing is controlled by a load balancer with percentage-based weighting

explanations.a: Comprehensive monitoring makes canary more effective — it enables faster detection of issues in the small canary traffic percentage.
explanations.b: If the new version runs a database migration affecting all users' data at startup — regardless of traffic routing — then even a 5% traffic canary has already modified the shared database that 100% of users depend on. The canary's traffic isolation provides no protection against shared resource side effects. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Automated rollback enhances canary deployment effectiveness — it makes recovery faster when issues are detected.
explanations.d: Percentage-based load balancer weighting is exactly the mechanism that implements canary traffic splitting — it makes canary more effective, not less.

questions.text: What is a rolling deployment strategy?

answers.text: Deploying the new version to all instances simultaneously causing a brief outage
Incrementally replacing instances running the old version with instances running the new version — a few at a time — until all instances are updated
Running two complete identical environments and switching all traffic between them
Sending 5% of traffic to the new version and gradually increasing the percentage

explanations.a: Deploying to all instances simultaneously describes an in-place deployment — rolling updates instances incrementally.
explanations.b: Rolling deployment updates instances in batches — a subset is taken offline, updated to the new version, and returned to service before the next batch is updated — maintaining continuous availability throughout the deployment. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Two complete environments with traffic switching describes blue-green — not rolling.
explanations.d: Percentage-based traffic routing to a new version describes canary — not rolling.

questions.text: A Kubernetes Deployment is configured with a rolling update strategy, maxUnavailable=1 and maxSurge=1 across 10 replicas. How does this control the update process?

answers.text: All 10 replicas are updated simultaneously — maxUnavailable and maxSurge have no effect

At most 1 replica is unavailable at any time and at most 1 extra replica is created above the desired count — ensuring at least 9 replicas serve traffic throughout the update while the 10th is being updated
Only 1 replica total will ever run the new version — the rest stay on the old version permanently
Updates only occur when all 10 replicas are idle

explanations.a: maxUnavailable and maxSurge directly control the rolling update pace — they are the primary controls for update speed and availability.
explanations.b: maxUnavailable=1 means at most 1 pod is taken down at a time, ensuring at least 9 are always serving traffic. maxSurge=1 allows creating 1 extra pod temporarily to replace the downed one faster. Together they control both availability and update speed. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Rolling updates eventually update all replicas — the process continues until every replica runs the new version.
explanations.d: Rolling updates occur on live running replicas — waiting for all replicas to be idle would be operationally impractical for production services.

questions.text: During a rolling deployment, monitoring detects failures on newly updated instances. The deployment is halfway complete — 5 of 10 instances are running the new version and 5 are running the old version. What is the PRIMARY risk of this state?

answers.text: The deployment automatically completes to minimize the mixed-version state duration

Users experience inconsistent behavior as requests are served by a mix of old and new version instances — some users get old behavior and some get new behavior, which can cause confusion, session issues, or data inconsistencies
The old instances refuse to communicate with new instances
The load balancer automatically routes all traffic to the old instances until deployment completes

explanations.a: A deployment with detected failures should pause or roll back — not automatically complete to minimize mixed-version duration.
explanations.b: A mixed-version environment means different users see different application behavior simultaneously. If versions are not backward-compatible, this causes session inconsistencies (a user whose session started on v1 is now served by v2), API response format differences, and data inconsistencies — a significant operational risk during rolling deployments. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Old and new instances can communicate on the same network — refusal to communicate is not a standard rolling deployment behavior.
explanations.d: Load balancers continue routing to all healthy instances regardless of version during a rolling deployment — they do not automatically revert routing on failure detection without explicit configuration.

questions.text: Which of the following BEST describes when rolling deployment is preferred over blue-green deployment?

answers.text: When instant rollback capability is the highest priority
When infrastructure cost is a concern and maintaining two full production environments simultaneously is not feasible — rolling deployment updates in-place without requiring double the infrastructure
When the new version contains breaking database schema changes
When the team needs to test two different versions with split user populations simultaneously

explanations.a: Instant rollback is blue-green's primary advantage — rolling deployments have slower rollback requiring re-updating instances.
explanations.b: Rolling deployment updates existing instances in batches without requiring a parallel duplicate environment — making it significantly more cost-effective for organizations that cannot afford to run double infrastructure during deployment windows. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Breaking schema changes complicate both rolling and blue-green — but blue-green is actually better for schema changes because you can validate the green environment against the new schema before switching traffic.
explanations.d: Testing two versions with split user populations describes canary or A/B testing — not the primary motivation for choosing rolling over blue-green.

questions.text: What is an in-place deployment strategy?

answers.text: Deploying the new version to a separate parallel environment before switching traffic
Updating the application directly on existing running instances — stopping the old version and starting the new version on the same servers
Gradually replacing old instances with new ones over time
Routing a percentage of traffic to a new version alongside the old

explanations.a: Deploying to a separate parallel environment describes blue-green — not in-place.
explanations.b: In-place deployment updates the application directly on the existing servers — stopping the running application, deploying the new version, and restarting on the same infrastructure without creating new instances or parallel environments. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Gradually replacing old instances describes rolling deployment — not in-place.
explanations.d: Routing percentages of traffic to different versions describes canary deployment — not in-place.

questions.text: A team performs an in-place deployment of a critical application update. The deployment fails and the application is left in a broken state. What is the PRIMARY challenge of recovery in this scenario?

answers.text: The previous version is still running alongside the broken new version

The previous version was overwritten during the in-place update — recovery requires either restoring from a backup, rolling back via package management, or redeploying the previous version from source control
In-place deployments automatically restore the previous version on failure
The load balancer continues to route traffic to the healthy old version while the broken instance is repaired

explanations.a: In-place deployment replaces the old version — they do not run simultaneously on the same instance.
explanations.b: In-place deployment overwrites the previous version on the same server. If the update fails, recovery requires restoring from a pre-deployment backup, using package rollback commands, or redeploying from source control — all slower and more complex than blue-green or rolling rollback. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: In-place deployments have no automatic rollback mechanism — rollback requires manual intervention.
explanations.d: In-place deployment updates all instances (not a subset) — there is no separate healthy old-version pool to route traffic to.

questions.text: A team is choosing between in-place and blue-green deployment for a non-critical internal tool with a 4-hour maintenance window available. The team has limited cloud budget. Which strategy is MOST appropriate and why?

answers.text: Blue-green, because it always provides the best deployment experience regardless of context

In-place deployment during the maintenance window — acceptable downtime tolerance and budget constraints make the simplicity and zero additional infrastructure cost of in-place appropriate for a non-critical internal tool
Canary deployment to gradually expose internal users to the new version
Rolling deployment to avoid any downtime during business hours

explanations.a: Blue-green requires double infrastructure — excessive cost for a non-critical internal tool with an available maintenance window.
explanations.b: For a non-critical tool where downtime during a maintenance window is acceptable, in-place deployment is the simplest and most cost-effective strategy — no additional infrastructure required, no complex traffic routing, and the 4-hour window provides ample time for deployment and basic testing. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Canary deployment is designed for production services requiring risk-limited rollouts to real users — overkill for a non-critical internal tool with an available maintenance window.
explanations.d: Rolling deployment avoids downtime but requires more complex orchestration than in-place — unnecessary complexity when downtime is acceptable during the maintenance window.

questions.text: An architect compares all four deployment strategies for a high-availability e-commerce platform that cannot tolerate downtime and requires fast rollback capability. Which strategy is LEAST suitable and why?

answers.text: Blue-green — because it requires double infrastructure
Canary — because it routes real users to potentially broken versions
In-place — because it overwrites the running application causing downtime during update and lacks fast rollback capability
Rolling — because it cannot maintain availability during updates

explanations.a: Blue-green's double infrastructure cost is a trade-off — but it satisfies zero downtime and fast rollback requirements, making it well-suited despite the cost.
explanations.b: Canary deliberately routes small percentages to new versions — it is designed for risk-limited exposure while maintaining availability.
explanations.c: In-place deployment stops the running application during update — causing downtime that violates the zero-downtime requirement. Recovery from a failed in-place deployment also requires redeploying the previous version rather than a simple traffic switch — making fast rollback impossible. This is the correct answer.
explanations.d: Rolling deployment maintains availability by updating instances incrementally — it satisfies the zero-downtime requirement even if rollback is slower than blue-green.

questions.text: What is the PRIMARY driver for most on-premises-to-cloud migrations?

answers.text: Cloud environments always provide better application performance than on-premises
Reducing capital expenditure, increasing scalability, eliminating hardware refresh cycles, and enabling access to cloud-native services and managed offerings
Cloud providers own the customer's data making it more secure
On-premises infrastructure cannot connect to the internet

explanations.a: Cloud performance is not universally better — it depends on workload type, network proximity, and configuration.
explanations.b: On-premises-to-cloud migrations are primarily driven by OpEx vs CapEx optimization, eliminating hardware procurement and refresh cycles, accessing elastic scalability on demand, and leveraging managed cloud services that reduce operational overhead. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Cloud providers operate under a shared responsibility model — data ownership and security remain the customer's responsibility.
explanations.d: On-premises infrastructure absolutely supports internet connectivity — this is not a migration driver.

questions.text: A company migrates their on-premises file server to cloud object storage. Users report that mapped network drives no longer work after migration. What is the MOST likely cause?

answers.text: Cloud object storage is faster than file shares so drives are no longer needed
Cloud object storage uses HTTP-based APIs rather than SMB/NFS file protocols — applications expecting mapped drive letter access cannot connect to object storage without a gateway or protocol adapter
The cloud provider blocked access to mapped drives for security reasons
Mapped drives require dedicated hardware that is not available in cloud environments

explanations.a: Speed is not related to drive mapping compatibility — the issue is protocol incompatibility.
explanations.b: Object storage exposes data through HTTP REST APIs — not the SMB or NFS protocols that Windows and Linux use for mapped network drives. Users expecting drive letter access need a file gateway (like AWS Storage Gateway) to translate between file protocols and object storage APIs. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Cloud providers do not block mapped drives as a security measure — the issue is protocol compatibility.
explanations.d: Mapped drives are a software/protocol feature — they do not require dedicated hardware.

questions.text: During an on-premises-to-cloud migration assessment, the team discovers several applications with undocumented dependencies on other on-premises systems. What is the MOST significant risk this presents?

answers.text: The cloud provider will refuse to host applications with undocumented dependencies

Migrating applications without mapping all dependencies can cause failures when the migrated application attempts to reach on-premises dependency systems it can no longer access — potentially causing cascading failures across interconnected applications
Undocumented dependencies automatically become cloud-native after migration
The migration cost will be exactly double for applications with undocumented dependencies

explanations.a: Cloud providers do not inspect application code for dependency documentation — this is not a rejection criterion.
explanations.b: Undiscovered dependencies are one of the most common causes of migration failures. An application that silently depends on a database, API, or service remaining on-premises will fail when moved to cloud if those dependencies cannot be reached — and the cascading failures can affect other applications that depend on the migrated one. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Migrating an application to cloud does not change its dependencies — it still requires access to the same systems it depended on before.
explanations.d: Cost estimation for migrations with undocumented dependencies is unpredictable — not a fixed multiplier.

questions.text: A company completes an on-premises-to-cloud migration and expects cost savings. Six months later, their cloud bill is higher than the previous on-premises cost. What is the MOST likely cause?

answers.text: Cloud providers intentionally charge more than on-premises costs

The migration was a lift-and-shift that replicated on-premises over-provisioning in the cloud — instances were sized for peak on-premises capacity and run 24/7 without right-sizing, reserved pricing, or cloud-native optimization
The cloud provider changed pricing after the migration completed
Cloud costs always exceed on-premises costs for enterprise workloads

explanations.a: Cloud providers compete aggressively on pricing — they do not intentionally charge more than on-premises.
explanations.b: Lift-and-shift migrations that mirror on-premises sizing into cloud instances — without right-sizing, reserved instance discounts, auto-scaling, or cloud-native optimization — often result in higher costs than expected because on-premises hardware is typically over-provisioned and fully depreciated while cloud instances are billed continuously at on-demand rates. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Pricing changes post-migration are not a common or primary cause of cost overruns — over-provisioning is the most frequent culprit.
explanations.d: Cloud costs can be significantly lower than on-premises when properly optimized — this sweeping claim is not accurate.

questions.text: What is a cloud-to-on-premises migration (also called cloud repatriation)?

answers.text: Moving workloads from one public cloud provider to another
Moving workloads that currently run in a public cloud back to on-premises infrastructure owned and operated by the organization
Migrating data from cold cloud storage to hot cloud storage
Moving development workloads from production cloud to a separate test cloud environment

explanations.a: Moving between cloud providers describes a cloud-to-cloud migration — not repatriation.
explanations.b: Cloud repatriation (cloud-to-on-premises migration) moves workloads from public cloud back to organization-owned on-premises infrastructure — the reverse of the typical cloud migration direction. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Moving data between cloud storage tiers is a lifecycle management operation — not a cloud-to-on-premises migration.
explanations.d: Moving between cloud environments (production to test) is a cloud-to-cloud operation — not cloud-to-on-premises.

questions.text: A company moves their database workloads from public cloud back to on-premises after discovering that cloud egress fees for their high-volume data transfer workloads are significantly higher than anticipated. Which migration consideration MOST directly caused this repatriation decision?

answers.text: The cloud database performance was insufficient for their workload
Unexpected cloud data egress costs for high-volume data transfer workloads made on-premises total cost of ownership more favorable
The cloud provider terminated their account without notice
On-premises hardware was newer than the cloud infrastructure

explanations.a: Performance was not cited as the issue — cost was the driver.
explanations.b: Cloud data egress fees are a common surprise for data-intensive workloads. Organizations that frequently move large volumes of data out of the cloud for processing or consumption often find that on-premises total cost is lower when egress fees are factored into the cloud TCO. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Cloud providers do not terminate accounts without cause or notice — this is not a realistic migration driver.
explanations.d: Hardware age is a capital investment consideration — not typically the primary driver for cloud repatriation.

questions.text: A company decides to repatriate workloads from cloud to on-premises. Which challenge is MOST significant during this reverse migration?

answers.text: Cloud providers make data extraction easy and free to encourage repatriation

Data extraction costs (egress fees), application dependencies on cloud-native services with no on-premises equivalent, procurement lead times for new hardware, and loss of cloud elasticity all present significant repatriation challenges
On-premises data centers cannot accept data transferred from cloud environments
Repatriation requires rewriting all application code from scratch

explanations.a: Cloud providers charge egress fees for data leaving their infrastructure — making large-scale repatriation costly, not free.
explanations.b: Repatriation faces multiple compounding challenges: paying egress fees to retrieve data, replacing cloud-native services (managed databases, serverless functions) with on-premises equivalents or rebuilding applications, waiting for hardware procurement and delivery, and permanently losing the cloud's elastic scaling capability. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: On-premises data centers can receive data from any network source — this statement is incorrect.
explanations.d: Repatriation does not require rewriting all application code — though applications heavily reliant on cloud-native services may require refactoring.

questions.text: A company's cloud-to-on-premises repatriation plan includes moving a workload that uses a serverless function and a managed NoSQL database. What is the MOST significant application consideration before repatriation?

answers.text: Ensuring on-premises servers have the same color LEDs as cloud hardware

The application relies on cloud-native managed services (serverless and managed NoSQL) with no direct on-premises equivalents — repatriation requires either self-hosting open-source alternatives or refactoring the application to work with on-premises infrastructure
Verifying that the on-premises network has the same IP address range as the cloud VPC
Ensuring the application's UI color scheme matches on-premises branding

explanations.a: Hardware aesthetics are irrelevant to migration planning.
explanations.b: Cloud-native services like serverless functions and managed NoSQL databases typically have no direct on-premises equivalents. Repatriation requires deploying self-hosted alternatives (e.g., running Apache Cassandra instead of a managed DynamoDB, or deploying containerized functions instead of serverless) — or refactoring the application architecture for on-premises compatibility. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: IP address ranges must be planned for network connectivity but are not the most significant application-level consideration.
explanations.d: UI branding is an application design concern unrelated to infrastructure migration.

questions.text: What is a cloud-to-cloud migration?

answers.text: Moving workloads from on-premises infrastructure to a public cloud provider
Moving workloads, data, or applications from one cloud provider's environment to another cloud provider's environment
Moving data between hot and cold storage tiers within the same cloud provider
Replicating workloads to a second availability zone within the same cloud region

explanations.a: Moving from on-premises to cloud describes an on-premises-to-cloud migration — not cloud-to-cloud.
explanations.b: Cloud-to-cloud migration involves moving workloads and data from one cloud provider (e.g., AWS) to another (e.g., Azure or GCP) — driven by cost optimization, vendor consolidation, better service fit, or avoiding vendor lock-in. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Moving data between storage tiers within the same provider is a lifecycle management operation — not cloud-to-cloud migration.
explanations.d: Replicating within the same region and provider is a high availability operation — not cloud-to-cloud migration.

questions.text: A company migrates from AWS to Azure. Their application uses AWS-specific services including DynamoDB, Lambda, and S3. Which challenge is MOST significant in this cloud-to-cloud migration?

answers.text: Azure does not support any workloads previously running on AWS

AWS-proprietary services have no direct Azure equivalents — the application must be refactored to use Azure Cosmos DB, Azure Functions, and Azure Blob Storage with potentially significant code and configuration changes
Data cannot be transferred between different cloud providers
The application's programming language must be changed to match Azure requirements

explanations.a: Azure supports a wide range of workload types — the challenge is service API compatibility, not fundamental workload support.
explanations.b: AWS-specific services use proprietary APIs and SDKs. While Azure has equivalent services (Cosmos DB ≈ DynamoDB, Azure Functions ≈ Lambda, Blob Storage ≈ S3), they are not API-compatible — requiring code changes to switch SDK calls, connection strings, and configuration. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Data can be transferred between cloud providers — it incurs egress fees and requires tools, but is technically feasible.
explanations.d: Azure supports the same programming languages as AWS — language changes are not required for cloud-to-cloud migration.

questions.text: A cloud-to-cloud migration team wants to minimize downtime during the transition from Provider A to Provider B. Which migration approach BEST achieves this?

answers.text: Take a full outage, migrate everything at once, and restore service on Provider B

Use a parallel running strategy — operate both environments simultaneously with data synchronization, gradually shift traffic to Provider B using DNS-based load balancing, and cut over completely only after validating Provider B fully handles production load
Migrate all data first, then migrate compute, accepting downtime between steps
Notify users of a 48-hour maintenance window and migrate everything sequentially

explanations.a: A full outage migration maximizes downtime — the opposite of the stated goal.
explanations.b: Running both providers simultaneously with synchronized data and gradually shifting traffic using DNS allows validation of Provider B under real load with instant rollback capability — minimizing risk and downtime during the transition. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Sequential migration of data then compute creates a window where data is on Provider B but compute is still on Provider A — causing application failures during that period.
explanations.d: A 48-hour maintenance window is excessive planned downtime — parallel running with gradual traffic shift avoids this.

questions.text: An organization performing a cloud-to-cloud migration discovers that their current provider charges significant data egress fees. How does this impact the migration plan?

answers.text: Egress fees are waived for cloud-to-cloud migrations by all major providers

Egress fees for moving large data volumes between providers can be substantial — the migration plan must account for this cost, potentially using physical data transfer services, staged migration, or prioritizing high-value data to minimize transfer volume
Egress fees only apply to migrations from on-premises to cloud — not cloud-to-cloud
Data egress fees are negligible for cloud-to-cloud migrations regardless of data volume

explanations.a: Egress fees are not waived for cloud-to-cloud migrations — providers charge for all data leaving their infrastructure.
explanations.b: Moving large data volumes between cloud providers incurs egress fees on the source provider's side. For petabyte-scale migrations, these fees can be substantial — requiring careful cost planning, potential use of physical transfer appliances (AWS Snowball), staged migration of the most critical data first, or data reduction strategies to minimize transfer volume. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Egress fees apply to all data leaving a cloud provider's network regardless of the destination — including cloud-to-cloud transfers.
explanations.d: Egress fees scale directly with data volume — they are significant for large cloud-to-cloud migrations.

questions.text: What is resource allocation in the context of cloud migration planning?

answers.text: The process of deleting unused cloud resources after migration
Determining and provisioning the appropriate compute, storage, memory, and network capacity in the cloud environment to support migrated workloads
Assigning team members to manage cloud accounts after migration
Allocating budget for cloud training and certification programs

explanations.a: Deleting unused resources is a post-migration optimization task — not what resource allocation during migration planning means.
explanations.b: Resource allocation in migration planning involves sizing cloud instances, storage volumes, and network capacity to match the resource requirements of workloads being migrated — ensuring performance requirements are met without over-provisioning. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Team member assignments are project management — not technical resource allocation.
explanations.d: Training budget allocation is a workforce planning activity — not cloud resource allocation.

questions.text: A migration team is allocating cloud resources for a database server that uses 8 CPU cores and 32GB RAM on-premises, with average utilization of 40% CPU and 60% memory. Which cloud instance allocation BEST balances cost and performance?

answers.text: Allocate an instance with 8 vCPUs and 32GB RAM to exactly match on-premises specifications
Rightsize the allocation based on actual utilization — select an instance with 4 vCPUs and 24GB RAM to match actual workload consumption while leaving appropriate headroom for spikes
Allocate the largest available instance to prevent any performance issues
Allocate 1 vCPU and 4GB RAM to minimize cloud costs

explanations.a: Matching on-premises specifications exactly replicates existing over-provisioning in the cloud — paying for resources that are consistently underutilized.
explanations.b: Rightsizing based on actual utilization (40% of 8 cores ≈ 3.2 cores, 60% of 32GB ≈ 19GB) selects an instance that comfortably handles actual load with headroom for spikes — optimizing cost while maintaining performance. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: The largest available instance would massively over-provision and generate unnecessarily high cloud costs.
explanations.d: 1 vCPU and 4GB RAM would be severely under-provisioned for a workload actually using 3+ cores and 19GB RAM — causing serious performance degradation.

questions.text: A migration team must allocate resources for a workload with highly variable demand — 10 instances at baseline and 200 during peak events twice a year. Which resource allocation strategy BEST handles this pattern?

answers.text: Reserve 200 instances year-round to guarantee peak capacity is always available
Reserve 10 baseline instances with reserved pricing, and use on-demand or spot instances for peak scaling — paying the premium cost only during the two annual peak events
Reserve exactly 105 instances as a compromise between baseline and peak
Use only spot instances for all 200 instances including during peak events

explanations.a: Reserving 200 instances year-round pays for 190 unused instances for roughly 363 days per year — extremely wasteful for twice-annual peaks.
explanations.b: Reserved instances for the stable baseline provides the maximum discount for consistent usage. On-demand or spot instances cover the peak demand — paying premium prices only during the brief peak periods when that capacity is actually needed. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: A fixed compromise of 105 instances over-provisions during 363 days and under-provisions during peak events — the worst of both approaches.
explanations.d: Spot instances can be interrupted — using them exclusively for critical peak events (where availability is essential) creates unacceptable risk.

questions.text: During a migration, a team discovers that the source on-premises environment uses local NVMe SSDs for database storage achieving 500,000 IOPS. They allocate a cloud instance with general-purpose SSD storage providing 10,000 IOPS. What problem will they encounter post-migration?

answers.text: No problem — cloud storage automatically matches on-premises performance

Severe database performance degradation — the migrated database requires 500,000 IOPS but the allocated storage only provides 10,000 IOPS — a 50x shortfall causing query timeouts and application failures
The database will automatically reduce its IOPS consumption to match available cloud storage
The cloud provider will automatically upgrade storage to match the workload's needs

explanations.a: Cloud storage performance must be explicitly provisioned — it does not automatically match on-premises hardware capabilities.
explanations.b: Allocating storage with 50x less IOPS than the workload requires will cause catastrophic performance degradation — I/O queues will back up, queries will time out, and the application will appear non-functional. Storage IOPS requirements must be assessed and allocated during migration planning. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Databases do not automatically reduce their I/O demands to match available storage capacity — they generate the I/O they need and fail when the storage cannot keep up.
explanations.d: Cloud providers do not automatically upgrade storage tiers — customers must provision the appropriate storage type and IOPS.

questions.text: Which storage consideration is MOST critical when planning a cloud migration?

answers.text: The color of the storage hardware used by the cloud provider
Assessing data volume, required IOPS and throughput, access patterns, latency requirements, and choosing the appropriate cloud storage type to match workload needs
Ensuring cloud storage uses the same RAID level as on-premises storage
Verifying that the cloud provider uses spinning disk rather than SSD

explanations.a: Hardware aesthetics are irrelevant to storage migration planning.
explanations.b: Storage migration planning must assess total data volume for capacity sizing, IOPS and throughput requirements for performance tier selection, access frequency patterns for tiering decisions (hot/warm/cold), and latency requirements — then match these to the appropriate cloud storage type. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: RAID configuration is a hardware-level redundancy concept — cloud storage handles redundancy differently through replication, not traditional RAID.
explanations.d: Cloud storage type selection is based on performance and cost requirements — specifying spinning disk vs SSD is not directly under customer control in managed cloud storage services.

questions.text: A migration team needs to transfer 2PB of data from on-premises to cloud within a 30-day window. Their internet connection is 1Gbps. Is internet-based transfer feasible, and what is the alternative?

answers.text: Yes — a 1Gbps connection can transfer 2PB in 30 days without issue

No — at 1Gbps, transferring 2PB would take approximately 185 days assuming 100% utilization. Physical data transfer appliances (such as AWS Snowball or Azure Data Box) shipped to the facility and loaded offline are the practical alternative
Internet transfer of 2PB is instant using cloud compression technology
The cloud provider sends a technician to connect a direct fiber link for large migrations

explanations.a: 1Gbps = ~10.8TB/day. 2PB ÷ 10.8TB/day ≈ 185 days — far exceeding the 30-day window.
explanations.b: At 1Gbps sustained, 2PB takes approximately 185 days — completely infeasible for a 30-day migration window. Physical transfer appliances loaded on-site and shipped to the cloud provider's facility can transfer petabytes in days. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Compression reduces data size but does not make 2PB transfer instantaneous over a 1Gbps link.
explanations.d: Cloud providers do not send technicians for direct fiber connections as a standard migration service — dedicated connectivity services exist but are not instantaneous.

questions.text: A cloud migration plan converts on-premises NFS file shares to cloud object storage. Post-migration testing reveals that an application cannot read previously written data because file paths are formatted as /data/reports/2024/Q1.xlsx but object storage uses key-based access. What migration preparation was missed?

answers.text: The application should have been tested in a pre-production cloud environment to identify path/key access pattern incompatibilities before production migration
The cloud provider should have automatically converted file paths to object keys
The application was too old to support cloud storage
Object storage does not support the .xlsx file format

explanations.a: Pre-production testing in the cloud environment would have exposed the incompatibility between the application's file path access patterns and object storage key-based access — allowing the team to implement a file gateway or refactor the application before production migration. This is the correct answer.
explanations.b: Cloud providers do not automatically translate file system path semantics to object storage keys — this is an application-level compatibility issue.
explanations.c: Application age is not the determining factor — the issue is the storage access API model incompatibility.
explanations.d: Object storage supports any file format including .xlsx — the issue is the access pattern, not the file type.

questions.text: A migration team must migrate a 50TB database while ensuring the source remains operational with minimal data loss during cutover. Which storage migration approach BEST achieves this?

answers.text: Take a full backup, restore it to the cloud database, then schedule a maintenance window for cutover with potential hours of data loss

Perform an initial full backup restore to the cloud database, then enable continuous database replication to keep the cloud target synchronized — cutting over with only seconds of data loss by stopping replication and redirecting traffic
Copy the data file by file using a standard file transfer tool while the database remains online
Export all data to CSV files and import them into the cloud database

explanations.a: A backup-then-cutover approach results in data loss equal to all transactions since the backup was taken — potentially hours of changes during a large database restore.
explanations.b: Initial full restore + continuous replication keeps the cloud target synchronized to within seconds of the source. Cutover only requires stopping replication and redirecting traffic — minimizing data loss to the replication lag, typically seconds or less. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Copying individual files while the database is online risks copying inconsistent or locked data — database files are not safely copyable during active operation.
explanations.d: CSV export/import is extremely slow for 50TB, loses database-native features, and cannot maintain synchronization during the migration period.

questions.text: Why is platform compatibility a critical consideration in cloud migration?

answers.text: All operating systems and applications run identically in cloud environments as on-premises
Applications may depend on specific OS versions, CPU architectures, kernel modules, or proprietary hardware features not available or behaving differently in cloud environments
Cloud providers require all applications to be rewritten before migration
Platform compatibility only matters when migrating between different cloud providers

explanations.a: Cloud environments virtualize hardware and may use different CPU microarchitectures, hypervisors, and OS configurations — not all applications run identically.
explanations.b: Applications may require specific OS versions unsupported in cloud images, depend on kernel modules unavailable in cloud kernels, use proprietary hardware like FPGAs not available as cloud instances, or rely on CPU instruction sets that differ between on-premises and cloud hardware. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Many applications migrate without rewriting — platform compatibility assessment determines which ones may need modification.
explanations.d: Platform compatibility is equally important when migrating from on-premises to cloud — not only in cloud-to-cloud scenarios.

questions.text: A company runs a 32-bit Windows XP application on-premises that is business-critical but cannot be updated. The migration team wants to move it to cloud. What platform compatibility challenge will they face?

answers.text: Cloud providers support Windows XP as a standard cloud VM image

Windows XP is an end-of-life OS with no cloud marketplace images — the team must bring their own licensed image, work around cloud driver compatibility issues for virtualized hardware, and may face security risks running an unsupported OS in cloud
The 32-bit application will automatically be converted to 64-bit by the cloud hypervisor
Cloud hypervisors cannot run 32-bit applications under any circumstances

explanations.a: Windows XP reached end-of-life in 2014 — cloud providers do not offer it as a marketplace image.
explanations.b: Running Windows XP in cloud requires bringing a licensed image, finding compatible virtual hardware drivers for cloud infrastructure, and accepting the security risks of running an unpatched, unsupported OS — a significant platform compatibility challenge for legacy applications. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Cloud hypervisors do not automatically convert 32-bit applications to 64-bit — the application binary architecture is unchanged.
explanations.d: Cloud hypervisors can run 32-bit applications — the issue is OS support, not architectural incompatibility.

questions.text: A migration assessment tool reports that 15% of an organization's applications have platform compatibility issues. Which action BEST addresses these applications in the migration plan?

answers.text: Migrate all 15% of incompatible applications immediately and fix issues post-migration

Categorize incompatible applications by severity and approach — some may be refactored to remove incompatible dependencies, others replatformed to compatible alternatives, and the most complex may be retained on-premises until a viable path is identified
Retire all 15% of incompatible applications immediately
Ignore compatibility issues and migrate all applications using the same approach

explanations.a: Migrating incompatible applications without resolving compatibility issues first risks production failures — post-migration fixes are more disruptive and costly than pre-migration remediation.
explanations.b: A tiered approach to compatibility issues is the industry standard — categorize by complexity and business impact, refactor where feasible, replatform where a compatible alternative exists, and retain on-premises where no viable cloud path currently exists. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Retiring business-critical applications solely because of compatibility issues would disrupt business operations — retirement requires a business decision, not just a technical one.
explanations.d: Ignoring compatibility issues causes production failures post-migration — compatibility must be addressed in the migration plan.

questions.text: A team migrates a Linux application that uses a specific kernel module (iptables extension) for custom packet filtering. In the cloud environment, the module causes kernel panics. What is the ROOT CAUSE and solution?

answers.text: The cloud network does not support iptables — replace all networking

The cloud hypervisor runs a different kernel version that is incompatible with the custom module — solutions include selecting a cloud image with a compatible kernel, recompiling the module for the cloud kernel version, or replacing the functionality with cloud-native network filtering
Linux kernel modules are not supported in any cloud environment
The application must be rewritten in a different programming language to avoid kernel modules
