explanations.a: iptables is widely supported in cloud Linux environments — the issue is kernel version compatibility, not a blanket networking restriction.
explanations.b: Custom kernel modules compiled for a specific kernel version may be binary-incompatible with a different kernel version running in the cloud VM — causing kernel panics when loaded. The fix requires either matching the kernel version or recompiling the module for the cloud kernel, or replacing the functionality using cloud-native tools. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Linux kernel modules are supported in cloud Linux VMs — the issue is version compatibility, not a categorical exclusion.
explanations.d: Programming language has no relationship to kernel module compatibility — this is a kernel/OS level issue.

questions.text: Which compute consideration is MOST important when sizing cloud instances for migrated workloads?

answers.text: Matching the exact number of physical CPU sockets from the on-premises server
Assessing actual CPU, memory, and compute utilization of the workload and selecting a cloud instance type that meets performance requirements without significant over-provisioning
Always selecting the highest performance instance type available
Matching the brand of CPU manufacturer used on-premises

explanations.a: Physical CPU sockets are a licensing concept for on-premises software — cloud instances use vCPUs and do not map directly to physical socket counts.
explanations.b: Right-sized compute allocation based on actual utilization metrics ensures the migrated workload performs adequately without paying for unused resources — balancing performance and cost. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Always selecting the highest performance instance maximizes cost unnecessarily — instance selection should match workload requirements.
explanations.d: CPU brand matching is irrelevant — cloud providers abstract hardware details and customers cannot specify CPU vendors.

questions.text: A migration team discovers that an on-premises application server runs at 90%+ CPU utilization during business hours with no autoscaling capability. How should compute be allocated in the cloud migration plan?

answers.text: Migrate to a cloud instance with the same CPU count as on-premises and accept the same high utilization pattern
Migrate to a larger compute instance and configure horizontal auto-scaling to add instances during peak load — distributing demand and preventing saturation
Reduce the instance size to cut costs since the application is already running slowly
Migrate as-is and rely on cloud providers to automatically optimize CPU allocation

explanations.a: Migrating to the same CPU count with 90%+ utilization perpetuates a known performance bottleneck — the migration is an opportunity to address this.
explanations.b: Migrating to a larger baseline instance for adequate headroom combined with auto-scaling to handle peaks eliminates the chronic CPU saturation while optimizing cost — only paying for additional capacity when actually needed. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Reducing instance size for a CPU-saturated workload would worsen performance — cost reduction should follow right-sizing, not arbitrary downsizing.
explanations.d: Cloud providers do not automatically optimize CPU allocation for customer workloads — instance sizing and scaling are customer responsibilities.

questions.text: A workload requires high-performance floating-point computation for scientific simulations that currently runs on an on-premises server with 4 Tesla V100 GPUs. Which cloud compute consideration is MOST critical for this migration?

answers.text: Selecting a general-purpose compute instance with the most vCPUs available
Selecting GPU-accelerated cloud instance types with equivalent GPU capacity and memory — verifying CUDA driver compatibility and GPU memory bandwidth meet the simulation's requirements
Using serverless functions to distribute the computation across many small functions
Using reserved memory instances with high RAM instead of GPU instances

explanations.a: General-purpose vCPUs cannot replace GPU floating-point performance for scientific simulations — GPUs perform parallel floating-point operations orders of magnitude faster than CPUs for this workload type.
explanations.b: GPU-accelerated instances with appropriate GPU count and memory, verified CUDA driver compatibility, and sufficient GPU memory bandwidth are the critical compute considerations for migrating GPU-dependent scientific simulations. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Serverless functions are CPU-based and have execution time limits — completely inappropriate for sustained GPU-accelerated scientific computation.
explanations.d: High-RAM instances optimize memory-bound workloads — they cannot replace GPU-accelerated floating-point computation for scientific simulations.

questions.text: A migration assessment shows that 20 on-premises servers run at average 15% CPU utilization with occasional 80% peaks during monthly batch jobs. Which compute strategy MOST optimizes cost while meeting performance requirements?

answers.text: Provision 20 large instances capable of handling 80% peak load continuously
Right-size baseline instances based on 15% average utilization with reserved pricing, and use scheduled or on-demand scaling to add capacity only during known monthly batch processing peaks
Consolidate all 20 servers onto 1 massive instance to reduce management overhead
Use spot instances for all 20 servers to achieve maximum cost savings

explanations.a: Provisioning peak-capable instances for 15% average utilization wastes resources 29 days per month — excessive and costly.
explanations.b: Right-sized baseline instances with reserved discounts handle normal 15% utilization cost-effectively. Scheduled scaling adds capacity only during the known monthly batch window — paying peak-capacity costs only when genuinely needed. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Consolidating all workloads onto one instance creates a single point of failure and does not handle distributed workload patterns — not a viable strategy.
explanations.d: Spot instances can be interrupted — unreliable for production workloads and particularly risky during critical monthly batch processing.

questions.text: Which cost consideration is MOST commonly underestimated in cloud migration projects?

answers.text: The cost of cloud instance compute hours
Data egress fees, licensing costs in the cloud, migration professional services, and the operational cost of running two environments simultaneously during migration
The cost of the internet connection used during migration
The cost of email communications during the migration project

explanations.a: Compute instance costs are highly visible and typically included in cost projections — they are not the most commonly underestimated component.
explanations.b: Migration projects consistently underestimate: data egress fees when moving large datasets, software license costs that change in cloud (BYOL vs. cloud licensing), professional services for migration tooling and expertise, and the cost of operating both source and target environments simultaneously during migration windows. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Internet connection costs are a known fixed monthly expense — not a commonly overlooked migration cost.
explanations.d: Email communication costs are negligible — not a relevant migration cost consideration.

questions.text: A company's on-premises software uses a per-socket server license that costs $5,000 per year. In the cloud, the vendor requires a per-vCPU license at $1,000 per vCPU per year. The workload needs 16 vCPUs. How does this affect the migration cost analysis?

answers.text: Software licensing costs are the same in cloud as on-premises

Cloud licensing at $1,000 × 16 vCPUs = $16,000/year vs. $5,000/year on-premises represents a 3.2x licensing cost increase — significantly impacting cloud TCO and potentially justifying BYOL, dedicated hosts for license optimization, or evaluating alternative software
Cloud providers cover all software licensing costs as part of the instance fee
The per-vCPU license cost is irrelevant since cloud instances are cheaper than on-premises servers

explanations.a: Software licensing models frequently change — and often increase — when moving to cloud environments.
explanations.b: The licensing cost increases from $5,000 to $16,000 per year — a 220% increase that fundamentally changes the migration TCO calculation. Licensing cost analysis is critical for any software with per-vCPU, per-core, or cloud-specific pricing models. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Cloud providers cover hypervisor and OS licensing for managed images but not third-party application software licensing.
explanations.d: Instance infrastructure costs and software licensing costs are separate line items — cheaper instances do not offset significantly higher software licensing costs.

questions.text: A cloud migration business case shows projected 40% cost savings. Six months post-migration, actual savings are only 5%. Which combination of factors MOST likely explains the gap?

answers.text: The cloud provider increased prices by 35% after migration

The business case assumed optimal rightsizing and reserved pricing but actual deployment used on-demand instances at on-premises sizing, combined with unexpected egress fees, licensing changes, and retained on-premises infrastructure that was not decommissioned
Cloud environments are inherently 35% more expensive than on-premises
The team incorrectly calculated the original on-premises cost

explanations.a: Cloud providers rarely increase prices post-migration — pricing is generally stable or decreasing.
explanations.b: The most common explanation for migration cost savings not materializing: on-demand pricing instead of reserved, lift-and-shift over-provisioning, unexpected egress fees, software licensing cost increases, and failure to actually decommission on-premises infrastructure (paying for both environments indefinitely). This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Cloud environments can be significantly cheaper than on-premises when properly optimized — a 35% inherent cost increase is not accurate.
explanations.d: While cost modeling errors occur, the more common issue is execution gaps — actual deployment choices diverging from the optimized business case assumptions.

questions.text: A migration team is evaluating three cost optimization levers for a cloud migration. Which combination provides the GREATEST long-term savings?

answers.text: Using on-demand pricing for all instances to maintain maximum flexibility

Rightsizing instances to match actual utilization, purchasing reserved instances for stable workloads, using spot instances for fault-tolerant batch jobs, and implementing auto-scaling to eliminate idle over-provisioning
Selecting the cheapest available instance type for all workloads regardless of performance requirements
Migrating all workloads to a single mega-instance to maximize per-instance utilization

explanations.a: On-demand pricing is the most expensive option — maximum flexibility comes at the highest per-unit cost.
explanations.b: Combining rightsizing (eliminates waste), reserved pricing (up to 70% discount on stable workloads), spot instances (up to 90% discount for fault-tolerant jobs), and auto-scaling (prevents idle over-provisioning) creates a comprehensive cost optimization strategy that addresses all major cloud cost dimensions simultaneously. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Cheapest instances without performance validation risks under-provisioning that causes application failures and business impact — false economy.
explanations.d: A single mega-instance creates a single point of failure, eliminates workload isolation, and provides no auto-scaling benefit — not a viable cost strategy.

questions.text: Which networking consideration is MOST critical when planning a cloud migration?

answers.text: Ensuring the cloud provider's data center is in the same city as the on-premises environment
Assessing connectivity requirements between on-premises and cloud, network bandwidth for data migration, IP address planning to avoid overlap, and security group and firewall rule migration
Verifying that cloud networking equipment uses the same vendor hardware as on-premises switches
Ensuring all cloud VMs use the same MAC addresses as their on-premises counterparts

explanations.a: Geographic proximity is one latency factor — but comprehensive networking planning covers connectivity, bandwidth, IP addressing, and security, not just location.
explanations.b: Networking migration planning must address: hybrid connectivity for the migration period (VPN or Direct Connect), bandwidth availability for large data transfers, IP address planning (avoiding CIDR overlap between on-premises and cloud VPCs), and translating on-premises firewall rules to cloud security groups and NACLs. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Cloud networking hardware is abstracted — customers do not interact with or specify physical networking equipment.
explanations.d: Cloud VMs receive new MAC addresses assigned by the hypervisor — on-premises MAC addresses cannot and need not be preserved.

questions.text: During migration planning, the team discovers the on-premises network uses the 10.0.0.0/8 address space and the cloud VPC is also configured with 10.0.0.0/8. What problem does this create and how should it be resolved?

answers.text: No problem — cloud networks automatically resolve IP address conflicts
Overlapping CIDR blocks prevent VPC peering and routing between on-premises and cloud — the cloud VPC must be reconfigured with a non-overlapping address range before establishing hybrid connectivity
The on-premises network must be renumbered to avoid the conflict
IP address overlap is only a problem for IPv6 — IPv4 overlap is handled automatically by NAT

explanations.a: Cloud networks do not automatically resolve IP conflicts — overlapping addresses cause routing ambiguity that prevents connectivity.
explanations.b: Overlapping CIDR blocks between on-premises and cloud VPC prevent the creation of VPC peering connections, VPN routing, and Direct Connect private connectivity — traffic cannot be routed because the network does not know which side a 10.x.x.x address belongs to. The cloud VPC must use a non-overlapping range. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Renumbering an existing on-premises network affects all devices and applications — reconfiguring the cloud VPC (which has no existing production traffic) is the less disruptive solution.
explanations.d: IPv4 overlapping addresses cause routing problems regardless of NAT — NAT can work around some overlap scenarios but is not automatic or a general solution.

questions.text: A migrated application experiences 10x higher latency accessing an on-premises database compared to the on-premises-to-on-premises baseline. The connection goes over a VPN through the public internet. What is the MOST effective solution?

answers.text: Upgrade the cloud instance to a larger compute type

Replace the VPN with a dedicated private connection (Direct Connect/ExpressRoute) to provide consistent, low-latency, high-bandwidth connectivity between the cloud application and the on-premises database
Move the database to the cloud to eliminate the hybrid connectivity latency
Increase the database query timeout values to accommodate the higher latency

explanations.a: Compute instance size affects processing capacity — it does not reduce network latency between cloud and on-premises.
explanations.b: VPN over public internet introduces variable latency and bandwidth constraints. A dedicated private connection (AWS Direct Connect, Azure ExpressRoute) provides predictable low-latency connectivity directly to the cloud provider's network — eliminating the public internet variability causing the 10x latency increase. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Moving the database to cloud is a valid long-term solution but represents a separate major migration effort — the immediate networking solution addresses the latency without requiring database migration.
explanations.d: Increasing timeout values masks the symptom without addressing the root cause — high latency still impacts user experience and application performance.

questions.text: A migration team needs to translate 200 on-premises firewall rules to cloud security groups. The on-premises firewall supports stateful inspection with rules referencing hostname-based FQDNs. What challenge does this translation present?

answers.text: Cloud security groups support more rules than on-premises firewalls so translation is straightforward

Cloud security groups typically only support IP address and CIDR-based rules — hostname/FQDN-based rules must be resolved to their current IP addresses, which may change over time, requiring either static IP commitments or implementation of DNS-aware firewall solutions
Cloud security groups are identical to on-premises firewall rule syntax
Firewall rules cannot be migrated to cloud — all security must be reimplemented from scratch

explanations.a: Security group rule limits exist per security group and per instance — not unlimited. More importantly, the translation challenge is functional, not a capacity issue.
explanations.b: Cloud security groups use IP addresses and CIDR blocks — FQDN-based rules require resolving hostnames to IPs at the time of rule creation. If those IPs change (dynamic DNS), the security group rules become stale and stop working — requiring either static IPs or DNS-aware security solutions like AWS Network Firewall. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Cloud security group syntax is specific to each provider's API — it is not identical to traditional firewall rule syntax.
explanations.d: Firewall rules can be migrated with appropriate translation — complete reimplementation from scratch is not required.

questions.text: How does cloud migration typically affect management overhead compared to on-premises?

answers.text: Cloud migration always eliminates all management overhead

Cloud migration shifts management overhead from physical infrastructure (hardware maintenance, data center operations) to cloud management tasks (cost optimization, security configuration, cloud service management) — the nature of overhead changes rather than disappearing entirely
Cloud management overhead is always higher than on-premises management overhead
Cloud providers manage all aspects of applications after migration with no customer involvement

explanations.a: Cloud migration eliminates some management tasks but introduces new ones — overhead does not disappear entirely.
explanations.b: Cloud migration trades hardware maintenance and data center operations overhead for cloud-specific management tasks: cost monitoring and optimization, IAM management, cloud security configuration, compliance in a shared responsibility model, and multi-service orchestration. The total overhead may be lower but is not zero. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Cloud management overhead is often lower in total but depends heavily on the services used and the team's cloud expertise.
explanations.d: Cloud providers manage their infrastructure — customers manage their applications, data, security configurations, and cloud resource usage.

questions.text: A company migrates from on-premises to a mix of IaaS VMs and managed PaaS services. Which statement BEST describes the difference in management overhead between these two approaches?

answers.text: IaaS and PaaS have identical management overhead — both are cloud services

IaaS VMs require the customer to manage OS patching, security hardening, and middleware — PaaS services offload these responsibilities to the provider, reducing per-service management overhead significantly
PaaS services require more management than IaaS because they have more configuration options
IaaS has no management overhead because the cloud provider handles all VM operations

explanations.a: IaaS and PaaS have fundamentally different management responsibility scopes — they are not equivalent in overhead.
explanations.b: IaaS customers manage the OS, middleware, runtime, and application — all layers above the hypervisor. PaaS customers manage only their application code and data — the provider handles OS, runtime, patching, and scaling. This significantly reduces per-service management overhead. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: PaaS typically has fewer management tasks despite more built-in features — managed services abstract away operational complexity.
explanations.d: IaaS customers have significant management responsibilities for VM-level operations — this is a defining characteristic of the IaaS model.

questions.text: A company migrates to cloud and initially saves on hardware costs but finds that cloud management overhead — monitoring dashboards, cost alerts, security compliance tooling, and multi-cloud management — requires significant engineering time. What does this illustrate about migration planning?

answers.text: Cloud migrations should be avoided because management overhead always exceeds hardware savings

Migration business cases must account for the cost of cloud management tooling, personnel training, and ongoing operational effort — infrastructure savings alone do not capture the full picture of cloud TCO
Management overhead only occurs in multi-cloud environments — single cloud deployments have no management costs
The engineering team should be replaced with automated systems that eliminate all management overhead

explanations.a: Cloud migrations can deliver significant savings — management overhead must be accounted for but does not necessarily negate the business case.
explanations.b: A complete migration TCO analysis must include cloud management tooling costs (monitoring platforms, security scanners, cost management tools), personnel training investment for cloud skills, and the ongoing engineering time for cloud operations — not just hardware savings vs. compute costs. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Single cloud deployments still require monitoring, cost management, security compliance, and operational management — management overhead exists regardless of the number of cloud providers.
explanations.d: Automation can reduce management overhead but cannot eliminate it entirely — human oversight of cloud operations remains necessary.

questions.text: A migration team chooses to use a cloud management platform (CMP) to centralize governance, cost visibility, and compliance across their multi-cloud environment. Which management overhead challenge does this MOST directly address?

answers.text: The overhead of physically maintaining server hardware across data centers

The overhead of using separate native consoles, billing dashboards, and compliance tools for each cloud provider — the CMP provides a single pane of glass for cross-cloud management, reducing tool proliferation and operational complexity
The overhead of writing application code for cloud-native services
The overhead of negotiating contracts with cloud providers

explanations.a: Cloud management platforms address software-layer management — not physical hardware maintenance.
explanations.b: Without a CMP, teams must use each provider's separate console, billing dashboard, IAM system, and compliance tools — multiplying management effort proportionally with the number of providers. A CMP aggregates these into a unified interface, dramatically reducing multi-cloud management overhead. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Application development is not an infrastructure management overhead concern — CMPs manage infrastructure, not application code.
explanations.d: Contract negotiation is a procurement activity — CMPs address operational management, not commercial negotiations.

questions.text: Why is service availability a critical consideration in cloud migration planning?

answers.text: Cloud providers guarantee 100% uptime for all services

The migration process itself can cause downtime — planning must address how to migrate workloads while minimizing service interruption and how cloud availability features differ from on-premises HA configurations
Service availability only matters after migration is complete — not during the migration process
On-premises services always have better availability than cloud services

explanations.a: No cloud provider guarantees 100% uptime — SLAs typically range from 99.9% to 99.99% depending on service and configuration.
explanations.b: Migration planning must address availability during the migration itself (minimizing downtime during cutover), and architect cloud deployments to achieve required availability SLAs using cloud HA features like multi-AZ deployment, load balancing, and auto-healing. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Service availability during migration is critical — the migration cutover is the highest-risk availability event in the migration lifecycle.
explanations.d: Cloud services can achieve higher availability than typical on-premises deployments when properly architected — neither is universally better.

questions.text: An on-premises application achieves 99.9% availability through a two-node HA cluster. After migration to a single cloud VM with no HA configuration, the team observes lower availability. What was missed in the migration?

answers.text: Cloud VMs automatically provide equivalent availability to on-premises HA clusters

The migration failed to replicate the HA architecture — a single cloud VM has no redundancy. The cloud deployment should use multiple instances across availability zones with a load balancer to match or exceed the on-premises availability level
Cloud providers guarantee higher availability than on-premises HA clusters
On-premises HA clusters cannot be replicated in cloud environments

explanations.a: A single cloud VM has no built-in redundancy — cloud VMs can fail just like physical servers.
explanations.b: The on-premises HA cluster's redundancy was not replicated in the cloud design. Cloud equivalents of on-premises HA require multi-instance deployment across AZs with load balancing — migrating to a single VM degrades availability rather than maintaining it. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Cloud availability depends on architecture — a single VM provides less availability than a two-node HA cluster.
explanations.d: On-premises HA patterns translate to cloud using multi-AZ deployments and managed load balancers — they are absolutely replicable in cloud.

questions.text: A business requires 99.99% availability for a migrated application. The target cloud region has had two outages in the past year affecting a single availability zone each time. Which architecture BEST meets the 99.99% requirement?

answers.text: Deploy the application to a single availability zone with a large instance type

Deploy across three availability zones with active-active load balancing, automated failover, and health checks — ensuring the application survives any single AZ failure and meets the 99.99% uptime SLA
Deploy to a single AZ and rely on the provider's SLA for the individual VM
Accept 99.9% availability since 99.99% is not achievable in cloud environments

explanations.a: A single AZ is a single point of failure — both historical outages affected individual AZs, making single-AZ deployment insufficient for 99.99%.
explanations.b: Multi-AZ active-active deployment with automated failover ensures the application continues serving traffic when any single AZ fails — the historical outage pattern confirms this is the right protection level for achieving 99.99% availability. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Individual VM SLAs are typically 99.9% at best — single-VM availability cannot achieve 99.99%.
explanations.d: 99.99% is achievable in cloud with proper multi-AZ architecture — many cloud-native applications exceed this availability level.

questions.text: A migration cutover is planned for a Saturday at 2 AM when traffic is lowest. Which activities are MOST critical to include in the cutover runbook to protect service availability?

answers.text: A list of team members' personal phone numbers

Pre-defined rollback criteria and procedure, health check validation steps for the cloud environment, DNS cutover timing and TTL pre-reduction, stakeholder communication plan, and a go/no-go decision point with specific metrics that must pass before completing cutover
A description of the cloud provider's data center architecture
The project budget remaining after migration

explanations.a: Contact information is important for escalation but is not the critical operational content of a cutover runbook.
explanations.b: A comprehensive cutover runbook must include: pre-defined rollback triggers (if X happens, roll back), validated health checks for the new environment, DNS TTL pre-reduction (to enable fast DNS-based rollback), stakeholder communication templates, and a formal go/no-go checkpoint with measurable success criteria — all essential for protecting service availability during cutover. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Provider architecture documentation is background knowledge — not operational runbook content.
explanations.d: Remaining budget is a financial tracking concern — not a service availability protection element of the cutover runbook.

questions.text: What is vendor lock-in in the context of cloud migration?

answers.text: A contractual obligation requiring customers to use a cloud provider for a minimum term
The degree to which an application becomes dependent on proprietary cloud services, APIs, or features that make it difficult or expensive to migrate to another provider in the future
A security feature that prevents unauthorized access to cloud resources
A billing model where customers pay in advance for cloud usage

explanations.a: Contractual minimum terms are commercial commitments — vendor lock-in refers to the technical difficulty of switching providers, not just contractual obligations.
explanations.b: Vendor lock-in occurs when applications are built using provider-specific services (proprietary databases, serverless runtimes, ML platforms) that have no standard equivalents — making future migration prohibitively expensive or technically complex. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Vendor lock-in is an architectural risk management concept — not a security feature.
explanations.d: Advance payment billing is a reserved pricing model — not vendor lock-in.

questions.text: A company chooses to use AWS DynamoDB, AWS Lambda, and AWS Cognito for their new cloud application. A consultant warns about vendor lock-in. Which specific risk does this warning address?

answers.text: AWS will automatically delete customer data if the company switches providers

All three services are AWS-proprietary — if the company needs to migrate to Azure or GCP, they must refactor significant application code to use equivalent non-compatible services, incurring substantial migration cost and risk
Using multiple AWS services is against cloud best practices
AWS charges extra fees when customers want to migrate to another provider

explanations.a: Cloud providers do not delete customer data when customers consider switching — this is not a real vendor lock-in risk.
explanations.b: DynamoDB, Lambda, and Cognito are AWS-proprietary services with no direct API-compatible equivalents on other providers. Migrating would require rewriting the application to use Cosmos DB, Azure Functions, Azure AD B2C — significant development effort and cost. This is the concrete lock-in risk the consultant identified. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Using multiple services from one provider is common practice — the risk is over-reliance on proprietary APIs, not the number of services.
explanations.d: Cloud providers charge egress fees for data transfer but do not impose specific migration fees — lock-in costs come from refactoring work, not provider-imposed penalties.

questions.text: Which architectural strategy MOST effectively reduces vendor lock-in risk while still leveraging cloud services?

answers.text: Using only open-source, container-based workloads with portable orchestration platforms (like Kubernetes) and avoiding proprietary managed services wherever feasible
Using every proprietary service offered by the cloud provider to maximize feature leverage
Deploying all workloads on-premises to completely avoid cloud lock-in
Using three different cloud providers for every workload simultaneously

explanations.a: Open-source workloads (PostgreSQL vs. DynamoDB, Kafka vs. Kinesis) running on portable Kubernetes can be moved between providers with minimal refactoring — significantly reducing lock-in risk while retaining cloud benefits like managed Kubernetes (EKS, AKS, GKE). This is the correct answer.
explanations.b: Maximizing use of proprietary services maximizes lock-in — the opposite of risk reduction.
explanations.c: On-premises deployment avoids cloud lock-in at the cost of losing all cloud benefits — not a balanced approach.
explanations.d: Using three providers for every workload creates massive operational complexity without necessarily reducing lock-in risk on any individual workload.

questions.text: A CTO argues that vendor lock-in is not always a concern and sometimes accepting it is the right business decision. Which scenario MOST supports this argument?

answers.text: When the company plans to migrate to a different provider every 6 months

When the proprietary service provides significant unique capabilities that dramatically accelerate time-to-market or competitive advantage — and the business value of those capabilities exceeds the estimated future migration cost and risk
When the company has no applications and no data in the cloud
When the cloud provider is financially unstable

explanations.a: Frequent provider switching maximizes migration costs — accepting lock-in for 6-month cycles makes no business sense.
explanations.b: Vendor lock-in is a business risk to be managed, not categorically avoided. When a proprietary service (e.g., a specialized ML platform) provides unique competitive capabilities that justify the future migration cost and risk — the business value calculation may favor accepting lock-in. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: A company with no cloud presence has no lock-in to accept — the scenario is irrelevant.
explanations.d: Financial instability of a provider is an argument against lock-in, not for it — diversification reduces risk from provider instability.

questions.text: Which environmental consideration is MOST relevant when migrating workloads from on-premises to cloud?

answers.text: The color of the server racks in the cloud data center
The reduction in on-premises power consumption and cooling requirements as physical servers are decommissioned — and evaluating the cloud provider's energy efficiency and use of renewable energy
The temperature of the office where the migration team works
The environmental impact of shipping server hardware to the cloud provider's facility

explanations.a: Rack color is irrelevant to environmental considerations.
explanations.b: Cloud migration can reduce an organization's environmental footprint by decommissioning on-premises servers — eliminating their dedicated power draw and cooling requirements. Additionally, large cloud providers operate at high efficiency with renewable energy commitments, potentially reducing overall carbon footprint per compute unit. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Office temperature is a workplace comfort consideration — not a cloud migration environmental factor.
explanations.d: Server hardware is rarely shipped to cloud providers — organizations decommission or repurpose their hardware locally.

questions.text: A company migrates all workloads to cloud and decommissions their on-premises data center. Which power and cooling consideration is MOST important to plan for during this transition?

answers.text: The company must maintain data center power and cooling indefinitely after migration

Power and cooling contracts, lease agreements, and utility commitments for the on-premises data center must be properly terminated — and any long-term contracts that outlast the migration timeline represent ongoing costs that offset cloud savings
The cloud provider pays the company's power bills after migration
Power and cooling costs transfer automatically to the cloud provider upon migration

explanations.a: Maintaining power and cooling for a decommissioned data center is wasteful — the goal is to terminate these costs after migration.
explanations.b: Data center power and cooling involve long-term contracts with utilities and facilities providers. If these contracts extend beyond the migration completion date, the company pays for both cloud infrastructure and empty data center power/cooling — directly reducing projected migration savings. Contract termination timing is a critical migration planning element. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Cloud providers do not pay customers' on-premises power bills — costs transfer to cloud compute billing, not to the provider.
explanations.d: On-premises costs do not automatically transfer — they must be actively terminated through contract and facility management.

questions.text: A company's sustainability team requires that all cloud workloads run on infrastructure powered by 100% renewable energy. How should this environmental requirement be addressed in the migration plan?

answers.text: All major cloud providers run 100% on renewable energy by default — no action is needed

Select cloud regions where the provider has committed to 100% renewable energy, use provider sustainability dashboards to track carbon emissions, and consider purchasing renewable energy certificates if the required regions do not have full renewable coverage
Deploy all workloads on-premises with on-site solar panels instead of migrating to cloud
Environmental requirements cannot be met by any cloud provider

explanations.a: Not all cloud providers run entirely on renewable energy in all regions — renewable energy coverage varies by region and provider.
explanations.b: Cloud providers publish sustainability commitments and carbon footprint data by region. Selecting regions with renewable energy commitments, using provider sustainability tooling to track emissions, and supplementing with renewable energy certificates for gaps gives the sustainability team verifiable environmental compliance. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: On-premises solar deployment is a separate sustainability initiative — cloud migration to green-energy regions is a valid and often simpler path to renewable energy use.
explanations.d: Multiple major cloud providers have strong renewable energy commitments and provide detailed sustainability reporting — this statement is incorrect.

questions.text: A company discovers that their on-premises data center PUE (Power Usage Effectiveness) is 2.5 while the target cloud provider's data center achieves 1.2 PUE. What does this mean for the environmental impact of migration?

answers.text: A higher PUE always means better environmental performance

PUE of 1.2 vs 2.5 means the cloud facility uses 52% less energy for cooling and overhead per unit of IT load — migrating to the cloud provider's infrastructure will significantly reduce energy consumption and carbon footprint per workload
PUE is irrelevant to cloud migration environmental considerations
The higher PUE of 2.5 means on-premises uses less energy per compute unit

explanations.a: Lower PUE is better — PUE of 1.0 would be perfect efficiency (all power used for IT, none wasted on overhead). Higher PUE means more energy wasted on cooling and infrastructure.
explanations.b: PUE = Total facility power / IT equipment power. A PUE of 2.5 means for every 1W of IT power, 1.5W is wasted on cooling and overhead. A PUE of 1.2 wastes only 0.2W per 1W of IT power — the cloud facility is dramatically more energy-efficient, directly reducing the environmental impact of running the same workloads. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: PUE directly quantifies data center energy efficiency — a key environmental metric for cloud migration decisions.
explanations.d: Lower PUE means higher efficiency — 1.2 is significantly more efficient than 2.5.

questions.text: Why must regulatory requirements be assessed before a cloud migration?

answers.text: Cloud providers automatically ensure compliance with all regulations without customer involvement

Regulations may dictate where data can be stored, how it must be protected, who can access it, and what audit trails must be maintained — non-compliance in the cloud environment can result in legal penalties regardless of where the infrastructure is hosted
Regulatory requirements only apply to on-premises infrastructure — cloud deployments are exempt
All cloud providers are certified to comply with every regulation globally

explanations.a: Cloud providers offer compliance-certified infrastructure but customers remain responsible for configuring their deployments compliantly — compliance is a shared responsibility.
explanations.b: Regulatory requirements follow the data regardless of where it resides. GDPR, HIPAA, PCI DSS, and industry-specific regulations impose data residency, encryption, access control, and audit requirements that must be implemented in the cloud environment or migration will create compliance violations. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Regulations explicitly apply to cloud deployments — regulatory bodies do not exempt cloud infrastructure from compliance obligations.
explanations.d: Cloud providers achieve specific certifications but not every possible global regulation — customers must verify which certifications cover their specific regulatory requirements.

questions.text: A company subject to GDPR wants to migrate EU citizen data to cloud. Which regulatory consideration is MOST critical to address?

answers.text: Ensuring the cloud instances use the latest hardware generation

Selecting cloud regions located within the EU to ensure GDPR data residency compliance, implementing encryption, configuring appropriate access controls, and ensuring the cloud provider has signed a Data Processing Agreement (DPA)
Migrating all data to the US as the primary GDPR compliance jurisdiction
GDPR only applies to on-premises storage — cloud is exempt

explanations.a: Hardware generation affects performance — not GDPR regulatory compliance.
explanations.b: GDPR requires EU citizen data to remain within the EU or jurisdictions with adequate protection, mandates encryption and access controls, and requires a Data Processing Agreement with cloud providers acting as data processors. All these must be configured in the cloud deployment. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: The US is not a GDPR-recognized adequate jurisdiction by default — transferring EU citizen data to the US without appropriate safeguards violates GDPR.
explanations.d: GDPR applies to all processing and storage of EU citizen data regardless of whether it is on-premises or cloud-hosted.

questions.text: A financial services company subject to both PCI DSS and SOX regulations migrates to cloud. Which regulatory challenge is MOST complex to address in a cloud environment?

answers.text: Selecting the correct instance type for financial calculations

Demonstrating continuous compliance to auditors in a dynamic cloud environment where infrastructure is provisioned and deprovisioned programmatically — traditional audit trails for hardware must be replaced with cloud-native logging, configuration management, and immutable audit evidence in cloud-managed services
Ensuring the cloud data center is in the same country as the company headquarters
Installing physical security cameras in the cloud data center

explanations.a: Instance type selection is a performance/cost concern — not a regulatory compliance challenge.
explanations.b: Dynamic cloud environments where resources are created and deleted automatically challenge traditional audit approaches. Demonstrating continuous PCI DSS and SOX compliance requires cloud-native audit logging (CloudTrail, Azure Monitor), infrastructure-as-code for configuration evidence, CSPM tools for continuous compliance monitoring, and cloud-adapted evidence packages for auditors. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Data residency requirements are relevant but not the most complex challenge — auditor evidence and continuous compliance in dynamic environments is more complex.
explanations.d: Physical security of cloud data centers is the provider's responsibility — not a customer installation task.

questions.text: A government agency operates under FedRAMP requirements and wants to migrate to cloud. Which action is MOST important during cloud provider selection?

answers.text: Selecting the cheapest available cloud region
Verifying that the cloud provider's services and regions are FedRAMP authorized at the appropriate impact level (Low, Moderate, or High) for the agency's data classification
Ensuring the provider offers the largest instance types available
Selecting a provider headquartered in Washington D.C.

explanations.a: Cost optimization is important but secondary to regulatory compliance eligibility for government agencies.
explanations.b: FedRAMP authorization is a prerequisite for federal agency cloud use. The provider must hold FedRAMP authorization at the appropriate impact level matching the agency's data classification — using a non-FedRAMP-authorized cloud service violates federal security requirements. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Instance type availability is a technical concern — not a regulatory compliance criterion for federal agency cloud selection.
explanations.d: Provider headquarters location has no bearing on FedRAMP authorization or compliance — authorization is based on security controls, not geography of the company's HQ.

questions.text: What is the difference between regulatory requirements and compliance in a cloud migration context?

answers.text: They are identical concepts with no distinction

Regulations are external mandates (laws and standards) that define what must be done — compliance is the ongoing process of implementing, monitoring, and demonstrating adherence to those requirements in the cloud environment
Compliance only applies to government organizations — private companies only have regulatory requirements
Regulations change daily while compliance is a one-time certification

explanations.a: Regulations and compliance are related but distinct concepts — conflating them misses the operational distinction.
explanations.b: Regulations define the rules (HIPAA, PCI DSS, GDPR). Compliance is the ongoing operational practice of implementing controls, monitoring their effectiveness, and providing auditable evidence that the organization meets regulatory requirements in its cloud environment. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Both private and government organizations face regulatory compliance obligations — industry regulations like PCI DSS and HIPAA apply regardless of organizational type.
explanations.d: Regulations change periodically and compliance is an ongoing continuous process — neither characterization in this answer is accurate.

questions.text: A company migrates a PCI DSS-compliant payment processing system to cloud. Which compliance activity is MOST critical post-migration?

answers.text: Updating the company website to mention the cloud migration

Updating the PCI DSS scope documentation to include the cloud environment and conducting a new assessment or Qualified Security Assessor (QSA) review to validate cloud compliance controls meet PCI DSS requirements
Notifying all customers about the infrastructure change
Removing all security controls that were specific to on-premises environments

explanations.a: Website updates are a communications task — not a compliance activity.
explanations.b: PCI DSS compliance is not automatically inherited when migrating to cloud. The cloud environment must be assessed and validated — the scope document must identify new cloud components, controls must be re-validated for the cloud deployment model, and a QSA review may be required to maintain compliance certification. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Customer notifications about infrastructure changes are not required by PCI DSS and are not a compliance activity.
explanations.d: Removing security controls invalidates compliance — cloud-specific equivalents must replace on-premises controls, not eliminate them.

questions.text: A cloud migration team wants to maintain compliance with HIPAA for healthcare data throughout the migration. Which approach to continuous compliance is MOST effective in a dynamic cloud environment?

answers.text: Run a manual compliance audit once per year and fix any issues found

Implement Cloud Security Posture Management (CSPM) tools that continuously scan cloud configurations against HIPAA controls, combined with infrastructure-as-code that enforces compliant baseline configurations and CloudTrail/audit logging for all PHI access
Store all PHI locally on team members' laptops to avoid cloud compliance concerns
Use only public cloud services as HIPAA compliance is the provider's responsibility

explanations.a: Annual manual audits miss continuous compliance drift — cloud resources are provisioned and modified dynamically, requiring continuous monitoring.
explanations.b: CSPM tools provide continuous automated scanning against HIPAA requirements, identifying misconfigurations in real time. IaC ensures compliant configurations from the moment resources are provisioned. Comprehensive audit logging provides the access audit trail HIPAA requires. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Storing PHI on laptops is a serious HIPAA violation — PHI must be protected with appropriate encryption, access controls, and audit logging.
explanations.d: HIPAA compliance is a shared responsibility in cloud — the provider secures the infrastructure, but the customer must configure services compliantly and protect PHI.

questions.text: During a cloud migration, a compliance officer discovers that cloud auto-scaling dynamically creates and destroys instances. This raises concerns about maintaining an accurate asset inventory required by their compliance framework. What solution BEST addresses this?

answers.text: Disable auto-scaling to maintain a static, easily inventoried environment

Use cloud asset inventory services and tagging combined with automated discovery tools that dynamically track provisioned resources — providing an always-current asset inventory without manual tracking of ephemeral instances
Manually update a spreadsheet each time an instance is created or destroyed
Compliance frameworks cannot be applied to dynamic cloud environments

explanations.a: Disabling auto-scaling eliminates a key cloud benefit to accommodate a manual process — the solution should adapt compliance tooling to cloud dynamism, not restrict cloud capabilities.
explanations.b: Cloud asset inventory services (AWS Config, Azure Resource Graph) automatically track all resources — including dynamically created and destroyed instances. Combined with mandatory tagging and CSPM, these tools provide an always-current, automated asset inventory that satisfies compliance framework requirements without manual tracking. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Manual spreadsheet updates cannot keep pace with dynamic cloud provisioning — compliance evidence would be perpetually outdated.
explanations.d: Modern compliance frameworks and their guidance documents explicitly address cloud environments — dynamic asset management is a solved problem with appropriate tooling.

questions.text: What does the rehost migration strategy involve?

answers.text: Redesigning the application architecture to use cloud-native services
Moving the application to cloud with no code changes — commonly called lift-and-shift — running the same application on cloud VMs as it ran on-premises
Replacing the application with a commercially available SaaS alternative
Partially optimizing the application by changing the runtime environment without changing the core application

explanations.a: Redesigning architecture to use cloud-native services describes re-architecting — not rehosting.
explanations.b: Rehost (lift-and-shift) moves the application to cloud VMs with no code changes — the application runs exactly as it did on-premises, just on cloud infrastructure. It is the fastest migration path but typically does not leverage cloud-native optimizations. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Replacing with a SaaS alternative describes the retire/replace strategy — not rehosting.
explanations.d: Changing the runtime environment without changing core application code describes replatforming — not rehosting.

questions.text: A company has 200 on-premises applications and must migrate to cloud within 6 months due to a data center lease expiration. Which migration strategy is MOST appropriate given the time constraint?

answers.text: Re-architect all 200 applications as cloud-native microservices
Rehost (lift-and-shift) the majority of applications to cloud VMs to meet the deadline — optimizing critical workloads for cloud-native features in a subsequent phase
Retire all 200 applications and purchase SaaS replacements
Retain all applications on-premises and negotiate a data center lease extension

explanations.a: Re-architecting 200 applications as microservices would take years — completely infeasible in a 6-month window.
explanations.b: Rehosting enables the fastest cloud migration by moving applications with no code changes — meeting the 6-month deadline while preserving the option to optimize afterward. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Retiring all 200 applications simultaneously would disrupt all business functions — not a viable approach without evaluated replacement options.
explanations.d: Retaining on-premises defeats the purpose of the migration mandate — if the data center lease expires, infrastructure must move.

questions.text: A team completes a rehost migration and the application runs in cloud but performance is worse than on-premises. What is the MOST likely explanation?

answers.text: Cloud infrastructure is always slower than on-premises hardware

The rehost migration copied on-premises over-provisioning patterns but missed cloud-specific optimizations — storage IOPS may be insufficient, network latency to on-premises dependencies has increased, or the application uses features optimized for bare-metal that perform differently in a virtualized cloud environment
The cloud provider intentionally throttles rehosted applications
Rehosted applications always perform worse — re-architecting is required for any performance

explanations.a: Cloud infrastructure can equal or exceed on-premises performance when properly configured — it is not inherently slower.
explanations.b: Rehost migrations that copy on-premises sizing without assessing cloud-specific factors — storage IOPS provisioning, network latency changes for hybrid dependencies, virtualization overhead for bare-metal-optimized applications — frequently experience performance regressions that require post-migration investigation and tuning. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Cloud providers do not intentionally throttle rehosted applications — performance issues have technical explanations.
explanations.d: Rehosted applications can perform well with appropriate cloud resource sizing — re-architecting is not a prerequisite for acceptable performance.

questions.text: Which of the following BEST describes the trade-off between rehost and re-architect migration strategies?

answers.text: Rehost takes longer but costs less — re-architect is faster but more expensive

Rehost is faster and lower risk but leaves cloud optimization benefits unrealized — re-architect takes longer and carries more risk but unlocks full cloud-native capabilities such as auto-scaling, managed services, and pay-per-use cost models
Both strategies have identical cost and time requirements
Re-architect is always required before rehosting can occur

explanations.a: Rehost is actually faster — re-architect requires more time for architectural redesign, development, and testing.
explanations.b: The core trade-off: rehost prioritizes speed and low migration risk at the cost of losing cloud-native benefits (auto-scaling, managed services, pay-per-use efficiency). Re-architect takes longer with more migration complexity but fully leverages cloud capabilities for long-term cost and operational efficiency. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Rehost and re-architect have fundamentally different time, cost, and risk profiles — they are not equivalent.
explanations.d: Rehost and re-architect are independent strategies — rehosting does not require re-architecting first.

questions.text: What is the replatform migration strategy?

answers.text: Moving an application to cloud with absolutely no changes to code or configuration

Making minor optimizations to the application during migration — such as moving to a managed database service or containerizing the application — without changing the core application architecture or code
Completely redesigning the application as cloud-native microservices
Replacing the application with a SaaS alternative

explanations.a: No changes at all describes rehosting — replatforming involves targeted optimizations.
explanations.b: Replatform (lift-tinker-and-shift) makes targeted improvements during migration — migrating a self-managed database to a managed cloud database service, or containerizing an application — gaining cloud benefits without the full effort of architectural redesign. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Complete architectural redesign describes re-architecting — not replatforming.
explanations.d: Replacing with SaaS describes retiring/replacing — not replatforming.

questions.text: A team migrates a web application from on-premises to cloud. Instead of managing the MySQL database on a VM (as done on-premises), they switch to Amazon RDS for MySQL. The application code requires no changes. This is an example of which migration strategy?

answers.text: Rehost
Replatform
Re-architect
Retire

explanations.a: Rehost makes no changes — switching from a self-managed VM database to a managed database service is an optimization change.
explanations.b: Switching from a self-managed MySQL VM to Amazon RDS (a managed service) with no application code changes is the textbook example of replatforming — a targeted cloud optimization without architectural redesign. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Re-architecting involves significant architectural changes — switching database hosting while keeping the same application code is not re-architecting.
explanations.d: Retire eliminates the workload — the application is being migrated, not retired.

questions.text: A team replatforms an application by containerizing it and deploying to Kubernetes. Post-deployment, the application cannot write temporary files to /tmp because the container runs as a non-root user with read-only filesystem. What does this illustrate about replatforming?

answers.text: Kubernetes does not support applications that write to the filesystem

Replatforming — even without architectural redesign — can expose application dependencies on runtime behaviors that differ in the new platform, requiring targeted fixes to application configuration or startup behavior
The application must be completely rewritten to support containers
Containerization always requires application code changes

explanations.a: Kubernetes supports filesystem writes — the issue is container security configuration, not a platform limitation.
explanations.b: Replatforming is not always seamless — moving to a container platform changes runtime assumptions (filesystem permissions, network addressing, environment variable injection) that on-premises applications relied on implicitly. Targeted fixes (volume mounts for /tmp, non-root-compatible file handling) are typically needed without full re-architecture. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: The fix is configuration-level (adding a writable volume mount) — not a complete application rewrite.
explanations.d: Many containerized applications require only configuration changes, not code changes — this statement overgeneralizes.

questions.text: A company replatforms 50 applications by migrating self-managed databases to managed cloud database services. After migration, the DBA team has significantly less work. What organizational implication should the migration plan address?

answers.text: The DBA team should be immediately replaced by the managed database service

The replatforming changes the DBA team's role — reducing infrastructure management tasks but increasing focus on database performance tuning, query optimization, schema design, and cloud database service configuration. The migration plan should address skills transition and role evolution
Managed databases require more DBA work than self-managed databases
The organizational impact of replatforming is irrelevant to the migration plan

explanations.a: Managed services reduce operational tasks but do not eliminate the need for database expertise — DBAs shift focus, not disappear.
explanations.b: Replatforming to managed database services eliminates patch management, backup configuration, and failover management from the DBA role — but database design, query optimization, and cloud service configuration remain critical. The migration plan should include role evolution planning and skills training to leverage the team's expertise in the new model. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Managed databases reduce DBA infrastructure workload — they do not increase it.
explanations.d: Organizational impact of technology changes is a critical people dimension of migration planning — ignoring it causes resistance and skills gaps.

questions.text: What is the re-architect migration strategy?

answers.text: Moving an application to cloud with no code or architecture changes
Fundamentally redesigning the application to use cloud-native architectures — such as microservices, serverless, managed services, and event-driven patterns — to fully leverage cloud capabilities
Making minor optimizations during migration without changing the core architecture
Retiring the application and replacing it with a SaaS solution

explanations.a: No changes describes rehosting — re-architecting involves significant redesign.
explanations.b: Re-architecting (also called refactoring in the context of architectural changes) involves fundamentally redesigning the application to take full advantage of cloud-native capabilities — decomposing monoliths into microservices, adopting serverless, using managed services, and implementing cloud-native patterns for resilience and scalability. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Minor optimizations without core architecture changes describes replatforming — not re-architecting.
explanations.d: Retiring and replacing with SaaS describes the retire/replace strategy — not re-architecting.

questions.text: A company's on-premises monolithic e-commerce application cannot scale to handle peak holiday traffic. They decide to re-architect for cloud. Which transformation BEST represents a re-architect approach?

answers.text: Moving the monolith to a larger cloud VM
Decomposing the monolith into independent microservices (product catalog, cart, payments, orders) each deployed as auto-scaling containers with managed databases and event-driven communication
Containerizing the monolith without changing its internal structure
Moving the monolith to a managed app hosting platform without code changes

explanations.a: Moving to a larger VM is a vertical scaling approach — it does not address the fundamental architectural limitations of the monolith.
explanations.b: Decomposing the monolith into independently scalable microservices with auto-scaling, managed databases, and event-driven communication is a complete cloud-native re-architecture that directly solves the scalability problem. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Containerizing the monolith without internal changes is replatforming — the architecture remains monolithic.
explanations.d: Moving to a managed hosting platform without code changes is replatforming — not re-architecting.

questions.text: A team re-architects a monolithic application into microservices for cloud. Three months after launch, they report that the microservices architecture has higher operational complexity than the original monolith. What does this illustrate about re-architecting?

answers.text: Microservices are always wrong for cloud deployments

Re-architecting to microservices introduces distributed system complexity (service discovery, distributed tracing, inter-service failures, network latency) that must be addressed with appropriate tooling and team expertise — re-architecting is not appropriate for all applications regardless of cloud migration
Re-architecting should always be performed before any other migration strategy
The increased complexity proves re-architecting was a mistake for any application

explanations.a: Microservices are valuable for the right applications — the issue is matching architecture to application characteristics and team capability.
explanations.b: Re-architecting to microservices trades monolith simplicity for distributed system complexity. This trade-off is worthwhile when the benefits (independent scaling, independent deployment, polyglot technology) justify the complexity cost. For applications where the monolith's limitations are not significant business problems, re-architecting may introduce more complexity than it resolves. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Re-architecting is the most expensive and time-consuming strategy — it is appropriate only when business requirements demand it, not as a prerequisite for other strategies.
explanations.d: The increased complexity is a predictable outcome of microservices — it does not categorically prove re-architecting was wrong, but it does confirm the team needed better preparation for distributed systems operations.

questions.text: Which application characteristic MOST strongly justifies the time and cost investment of re-architecting rather than rehosting during cloud migration?

answers.text: The application has fewer than 10 users

The application has significant scalability limitations in its current architecture that prevent it from meeting business growth requirements — and cloud-native re-architecture would unlock capabilities (auto-scaling, managed services, event-driven processing) that directly address those limitations
The application was written more than 5 years ago
The application's UI uses an older CSS framework

explanations.a: Low user count suggests limited scalability pressure — re-architecting for a 10-user application would likely not justify the investment.
explanations.b: Re-architecting is justified when the current architecture has fundamental limitations (can only scale vertically, cannot handle elastic demand, creates operational bottlenecks) that only a cloud-native redesign can solve. The business value of unlocking those capabilities must exceed the re-architecting investment. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Application age alone does not justify re-architecting — the architecture's ability to meet current and future requirements is the relevant factor.
explanations.d: UI framework choice is a front-end development concern — not a justification for a fundamental cloud re-architecture effort.

questions.text: What does the retain migration strategy mean?

answers.text: Migrating the application to cloud immediately regardless of readiness
Keeping the application on-premises and not migrating it — intentionally deciding it should remain where it is for now
Retaining the application's code but rewriting the infrastructure layer
Keeping a copy of the application on-premises while migrating a second copy to cloud

explanations.a: Migrating immediately is the opposite of retain — retain is a deliberate decision not to migrate.
explanations.b: Retain is a deliberate migration assessment decision to keep a specific application on-premises — typically because it has complex dependencies that make migration risky, requires specialized hardware unavailable in cloud, or is scheduled for retirement making migration investment unjustifiable. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Rewriting the infrastructure layer while keeping code describes replatforming — not retain.
explanations.d: Keeping an on-premises copy while also deploying to cloud describes a hybrid approach — not a retain strategy.

questions.text: A migration assessment identifies an on-premises ERP system that is scheduled for replacement with a new SaaS ERP in 12 months. The current ERP has complex dependencies making it difficult to migrate. Which strategy is MOST appropriate?

answers.text: Rehost the ERP to cloud immediately to gain early migration credit
Retain the ERP on-premises for the remaining 12 months — migrating it would be wasteful given the planned replacement — and focus migration resources on applications with longer lifespans
Re-architect the ERP as microservices before it is replaced
Retire the ERP immediately and use the new SaaS solution before it is ready

explanations.a: Migrating an application 12 months from planned replacement wastes migration resources — the effort of migration exceeds its useful lifetime in cloud.
explanations.b: With a 12-month replacement timeline and complex dependencies, retaining the ERP on-premises is the most cost-effective decision — avoiding migration costs for a system that will be replaced before migration investment is recouped. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Re-architecting a system scheduled for replacement within 12 months wastes even more resources — the architectural investment would be discarded at replacement.
explanations.d: Retiring before a replacement is ready disrupts business operations — retire decisions require replacement readiness.

questions.text: A migration team initially retains 30 on-premises applications due to complexity. Six months later, the cloud migration is complete but the 30 retained applications still require on-premises infrastructure. What long-term risk does this situation create?

answers.text: No risk — retaining applications is always the best strategy

Retained applications prevent full data center decommissioning — the organization continues bearing on-premises infrastructure costs (hardware, power, cooling, staff) indefinitely, potentially negating the cost savings of the cloud migration for other applications
Retained applications automatically migrate themselves to cloud over time
Retained applications have no impact on cloud migration cost savings

explanations.a: Retain is a deliberate decision — but retaining too many applications creates a permanent hybrid estate that prevents full cost optimization.
explanations.b: Retained applications require ongoing on-premises infrastructure — preventing data center decommissioning and its associated savings. If enough applications are retained, the organization pays for both cloud and on-premises infrastructure indefinitely, significantly reducing net migration savings. Retained applications need a roadmap to eventually migrate, retire, or replace. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Retained applications require active migration planning — they do not self-migrate.
explanations.d: Retained applications directly impact cost savings by preserving on-premises infrastructure costs that should have been eliminated.

questions.text: Which application characteristic MOST justifies a retain decision during a cloud migration assessment?

answers.text: The application is widely used by 500 employees daily
The application requires specialized hardware (e.g., a specific FPGA card or proprietary I/O device) with no cloud equivalent, making migration technically infeasible without hardware replacement
The application was developed internally by the company's software team
The application uses a relational database

explanations.a: High usage is a reason to migrate to gain cloud benefits — not a justification for retention.
explanations.b: Hardware dependency on devices with no cloud equivalent is a technically valid retain justification — if the application requires a specific FPGA card, custom USB interface, or proprietary hardware that cannot be replicated in cloud, the application must remain on-premises until a cloud-compatible alternative exists. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Internal development is not a migration barrier — the team can migrate internally-developed applications.
explanations.d: Relational databases are well-supported in cloud — they are not a retain justification.

questions.text: What does the retire migration strategy mean?

answers.text: Moving the application to cloud and running it alongside a newer replacement
Decommissioning the application entirely — turning it off and removing it from the portfolio because it is no longer needed or has been replaced by another solution
Temporarily suspending the application during the migration window
Migrating the application to cloud and then decommissioning the on-premises version

explanations.a: Running alongside a replacement would be a transition approach — retire means the application is permanently discontinued.
explanations.b: Retire means the application is decommissioned and removed from the portfolio — no migration occurs because the application has reached end-of-life, its users have moved to alternative solutions, or it no longer serves any business function. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Temporary suspension is an operational action — retire is a permanent decommissioning decision.
explanations.d: Decommissioning the on-premises version after cloud migration describes a successful rehost — retire involves not migrating at all and simply turning the application off.

questions.text: During a migration assessment, the team discovers an application that has 3 active users out of 500 originally provisioned licenses, has not been updated in 4 years, and duplicates functionality already available in the company's CRM system. Which strategy should be applied?

answers.text: Rehost the application to cloud to preserve it for potential future use
Retire the application — minimal active usage, duplicate functionality, and extended inactivity indicate the application provides no business value worth the cost of migration or ongoing maintenance
Re-architect the application as microservices to modernize it
Retain the application on-premises indefinitely

explanations.a: Rehosting an application with 3 users, duplicate functionality, and zero recent updates wastes migration resources on a system with no meaningful business value.
explanations.b: An application with 3 active users, 4 years without updates, and duplicate CRM functionality has clear retirement indicators — low utilization, functional redundancy, and technical stagnation. Retiring it reduces portfolio complexity and eliminates ongoing maintenance cost. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Re-architecting a low-value application with 3 users is an unjustifiable investment.
explanations.d: Retaining an application that should be retired continues unnecessary infrastructure and maintenance costs.

questions.text: A migration team identifies 15% of the application portfolio as candidates for retirement. What must be done BEFORE actually retiring these applications?

answers.text: Immediately shut down all 15% of applications on the first day of the migration project

Validate with business stakeholders that the applications are truly no longer needed, confirm no hidden dependencies on other systems, archive data per retention policies, notify all users, and obtain formal business sign-off before decommissioning
Migrate the applications to cloud first and then retire them
Publish a company-wide announcement that the applications will be retired in 24 hours

explanations.a: Immediately shutting down applications without validation risks disrupting business functions that still depend on them — even low-usage applications may have critical undiscovered dependencies.
explanations.b: Retirement requires business validation (confirming no active need), dependency mapping (ensuring no other system silently depends on the retiring application), data archiving per retention policies (data may have legal hold requirements), user notification (allowing transition time), and formal sign-off — all essential steps before decommissioning. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Migrating applications only to immediately retire them wastes migration effort — retire decisions should be made before migration planning begins.
explanations.d: 24-hour notice for application retirement is insufficient — users and dependent teams need adequate transition time.

questions.text: A migration team retires 40 applications, expecting to eliminate all associated on-premises infrastructure. After retirement, 15 of the 40 servers remain in use. What does this reveal about the retirement process?

answers.text: The 15 servers have self-provisioned new applications automatically

The dependency mapping was incomplete — 15 servers were hosting other applications, services, or scheduled jobs that were not identified during the retirement assessment, preventing full decommissioning
Cloud providers require keeping some on-premises servers active after application retirement
Server hardware cannot be decommissioned after application retirement

explanations.a: Servers do not self-provision applications — this is not a realistic scenario.
explanations.b: Remaining active servers after retirement indicate undiscovered applications, services, batch jobs, or system agents running on those servers that were not captured during the dependency assessment. Complete server decommissioning requires fully mapping all workloads on each physical machine — not just the primary applications being retired. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Cloud providers do not require customers to maintain on-premises servers — this statement is incorrect.
explanations.d: Server hardware can absolutely be decommissioned after all workloads are removed — remaining activity prevents decommissioning.

questions.text: In the context of cloud migration strategies, what does refactor mean?

answers.text: Moving an application to cloud with no code changes

Making targeted code-level changes to optimize the application for cloud — such as externalizing configuration, removing hard-coded paths, or adopting cloud-compatible libraries — without completely redesigning the architecture
Completely redesigning the application as cloud-native microservices from scratch
