Six months later, they discover the provisioned general-purpose SSD cannot meet the database's 50,000 IOPS demand and throughput is throttled. What should have been done during provisioning?

answers.text: Always provision the cheapest storage option and upgrade later if needed
Assessed the application's IOPS and throughput requirements before provisioning and selected provisioned IOPS SSD storage with the appropriate performance tier to meet the workload's peak demands
Provisioned archive storage for the database to minimize upfront costs
Used object storage instead of block storage for the database

explanations.a: Provisioning cheapest-first for performance-sensitive workloads causes production throttling — requirements must be assessed before provisioning.
explanations.b: Database IOPS requirements must be measured or estimated during provisioning planning. Selecting provisioned IOPS SSD with sufficient IOPS allocation prevents the throttling issue — storage performance is not easily changed after provisioning without downtime. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Archive storage has negligible IOPS — completely unsuitable for an active database.
explanations.d: Object storage uses HTTP APIs and cannot serve as a block device for database engines.

questions.text: A company provisions cloud storage for a machine learning training pipeline. The pipeline reads 5TB of training data sequentially each run and writes model checkpoints every 30 minutes. Which storage configuration BEST meets these distinct requirements?

answers.text: A single general-purpose SSD for both training data and checkpoints

High-throughput object storage or NFS for sequential training data reads (optimized for large sequential throughput) combined with a separate fast SSD volume for frequent checkpoint writes (optimized for low-latency random writes)
Archive storage for training data and object storage for checkpoints
A single HDD volume for all storage needs to minimize cost

explanations.a: A single general-purpose SSD handles neither the sequential read throughput of 5TB training data nor the high-frequency checkpoint write requirements optimally.
explanations.b: Training data requires maximum sequential read throughput — object storage or high-throughput NFS optimized for large sequential reads. Checkpoints require low-latency random writes — a fast SSD volume. Matching storage type to each workload's I/O pattern maximizes performance for both. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Archive storage has retrieval delays incompatible with frequent training data access — the pipeline would stall waiting for data retrieval.
explanations.d: A single HDD provides insufficient sequential throughput for 5TB reads and has high random write latency for frequent checkpoints.

questions.text: During cloud resource provisioning, a team debates between provisioning exactly the required storage capacity versus over-provisioning by 50% as a buffer. Which approach BEST balances cost and operational risk?

answers.text: Always over-provision by 50% regardless of growth projections

Provision based on current requirements plus a reasonable growth buffer (typically 20-30%), implement storage monitoring with capacity alerts, and configure auto-scaling or elastic storage services that expand dynamically — avoiding both over-provisioning waste and capacity shortage risk
Provision exactly the minimum required with no growth buffer
Provision 10x the required storage to guarantee no capacity issues for 10 years

explanations.a: A fixed 50% buffer without growth data may significantly over-provision for slowly growing workloads — wasteful and expensive.
explanations.b: Right-sized provisioning with a modest growth buffer, monitoring with proactive alerts, and elastic storage that scales automatically prevents both storage waste and unexpected capacity exhaustion — the balanced approach to storage provisioning. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Exact-minimum provisioning creates immediate capacity shortage risk when data grows — proactive buffer is necessary.
explanations.d: 10x provisioning for 10 years pays for years of unused capacity — economically unjustifiable in cloud where storage can be elastically expanded.

questions.text: Which performance requirements MUST be understood before provisioning cloud compute and storage resources?

answers.text: The team's preferred programming language and code editor
CPU utilization targets, memory requirements, IOPS and throughput demands, network bandwidth needs, and latency thresholds — determining appropriate instance types and storage tiers
The number of years the application has been in production
The preferred brand of hardware used on-premises

explanations.a: Programming language and editor preferences are developer tool choices — not cloud provisioning requirements.
explanations.b: Performance requirements drive all provisioning decisions: CPU needs determine instance family (compute-optimized, general-purpose, memory-optimized), IOPS requirements select storage type, network throughput determines instance network performance class, and latency thresholds may require placement groups or edge deployment. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Application age provides context but is not a direct performance requirement.
explanations.d: Cloud providers abstract hardware — on-premises hardware brand preference is not a provisioning criterion.

questions.text: A team provisions cloud resources for a real-time fraud detection system that must process 100,000 transactions per second with sub-10ms response time. Which provisioning decisions are MOST critical?

answers.text: Select the cheapest available instance type and standard HDD storage

Select compute-optimized instances with high vCPU counts, in-memory caching (Redis/ElastiCache) for fast lookup, NVMe or provisioned IOPS SSD storage, enhanced networking for sub-millisecond inter-service latency, and multi-AZ deployment for availability
Use serverless functions with a 15-minute timeout for fraud analysis
Use spot instances for cost savings on the fraud detection workload

explanations.a: Cheap instances and HDD storage cannot deliver 100,000 TPS with sub-10ms response — performance requirements demand high-performance infrastructure.
explanations.b: Compute-optimized instances handle high CPU throughput, in-memory caching provides sub-millisecond data access, provisioned IOPS storage eliminates storage bottlenecks, enhanced networking reduces inter-service latency, and multi-AZ ensures availability — all necessary for the performance profile. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Serverless functions have cold start latency and execution time limits incompatible with sub-10ms consistent real-time processing.
explanations.d: Spot instances can be interrupted — a fraud detection system cannot tolerate random instance termination mid-transaction.

questions.text: A team provisions a cloud database but performance testing reveals p99 query latency of 500ms versus the required 50ms. The database CPU is at 15% and memory is 20% utilized. What performance bottleneck should they investigate first?

answers.text: Add more vCPUs to the database instance

Investigate storage I/O — low CPU and memory utilization with high latency typically indicates an I/O bottleneck. Check IOPS utilization, storage queue depth, and whether the provisioned IOPS tier is sufficient for the query patterns
Add more RAM to the database instance
Move the database to a serverless tier

explanations.a: CPU at 15% is not the bottleneck — adding vCPUs would not address a non-CPU constraint.
explanations.b: When CPU and memory are low but latency is high, the bottleneck is typically I/O. High storage queue depth or insufficient IOPS means queries wait for disk operations — the classic I/O-bound performance profile. Upgrading to higher IOPS provisioning or NVMe storage directly targets the bottleneck. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Memory at 20% is not the bottleneck — adding RAM would not reduce I/O wait time.
explanations.d: Moving to serverless does not resolve an I/O bottleneck — it changes the billing model, not the underlying performance issue.

questions.text: An architect must provision cloud resources for a workload with performance requirements that include 200Gbps network throughput between instances in the same cluster. Which provisioning option BEST meets this requirement?

answers.text: Standard instances connected through the cloud provider's default virtual network
High-performance compute instances in a cluster placement group with enhanced networking (EFA/ENA) enabling high-bandwidth, low-latency inter-instance communication within the same availability zone
Instances connected through a VPN tunnel for secure high-bandwidth communication
Standard spot instances distributed across multiple availability zones

explanations.a: Standard instances on default virtual networks typically provide 10-25Gbps maximum — far below 200Gbps requirements.
explanations.b: Cluster placement groups co-locate instances on the same physical network hardware, and Elastic Fabric Adapter (EFA) or enhanced networking provides high-bandwidth, low-latency inter-instance connectivity achieving 100-200Gbps for HPC workloads. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: VPN tunnels add encryption overhead and are not designed for 200Gbps intra-cluster traffic — they would be a severe bottleneck.
explanations.d: Spot instances across multiple AZs have cross-AZ network latency and bandwidth limitations that prevent 200Gbps cluster networking.

questions.text: Which security requirements MUST be addressed when provisioning cloud resources?

answers.text: The physical appearance of the cloud provider's security badges

Encryption at rest and in transit, IAM access controls implementing least privilege, network security groups and firewall rules, audit logging, and vulnerability management — configured at resource creation rather than added after deployment
The number of security engineers who will monitor the resources
The vendor's office security procedures

explanations.a: Physical badge appearance is irrelevant to cloud resource security requirements.
explanations.b: Security must be built into provisioning — not bolted on afterward. Encryption, least-privilege IAM, network security rules, and audit logging configured at resource creation ensure a secure baseline from day one. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Security staff count is an organizational consideration — not a provisioning requirement for specific resources.
explanations.d: Vendor office security is the provider's physical security responsibility — not a customer provisioning requirement.

questions.text: A team provisions a cloud database containing sensitive customer PII. Which security requirements MUST be implemented at provisioning time?

answers.text: Enable server-side encryption using customer-managed keys, restrict database access to the application subnet only via security groups, enable audit logging for all database queries, and implement IAM authentication rather than static passwords
Make the database publicly accessible for easy developer access during initial setup
Use default AWS-managed encryption and allow access from any IP address
Disable logging to prevent performance overhead on the database

explanations.a: Customer-managed encryption keys provide data control, subnet-restricted security groups limit access surface, audit logging enables compliance, and IAM authentication eliminates static credential risks — all appropriate for PII database provisioning. This is the correct answer.
explanations.b: Public database access exposes PII to the internet — a critical security violation for any sensitive data store.
explanations.c: Default encryption is a start but allowing access from any IP violates least-privilege network security for PII data.
explanations.d: Disabling audit logging for a PII database eliminates the access audit trail required by GDPR, HIPAA, and most data protection regulations.

questions.text: An architect provisions cloud resources for a multi-tier application. The security requirement specifies that the web tier, application tier, and database tier must be completely isolated from each other with only explicitly permitted traffic flows between adjacent tiers. Which provisioning approach implements this?

answers.text: Deploy all three tiers in the same subnet with no security groups configured

Provision each tier in separate subnets with dedicated security groups that only allow traffic from the adjacent tier — web tier security group allows inbound from internet load balancer, app tier security group allows inbound only from web tier security group, database security group allows inbound only from app tier security group
Provision all tiers with identical security groups allowing all inbound traffic for simplicity
Deploy all three tiers in a single VM to eliminate inter-tier network security requirements

explanations.a: No security groups means no enforced isolation — any tier can communicate with any other freely.
explanations.b: Tiered subnet segmentation with security groups referencing upstream security group IDs implements strict least-privilege traffic flow: internet → load balancer → web tier → app tier → database, with no tier able to bypass its neighbors or communicate with non-adjacent tiers. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Identical security groups allowing all inbound traffic provide no isolation — all tiers can communicate freely with each other and potentially the internet.
explanations.d: Single VM deployment eliminates network isolation entirely and creates a single point of failure — not a valid security architecture.

questions.text: During provisioning, a team is deciding between AWS-managed encryption keys (SSE-S3) and customer-managed keys (SSE-KMS) for S3 bucket encryption. A regulatory requirement states the organization must control key rotation and be able to revoke access to encrypted data immediately. Which option meets this requirement?

answers.text: SSE-S3 with AWS-managed keys because it is simpler and requires no customer management
SSE-KMS with customer-managed keys because the organization controls key rotation schedules, can audit key usage, and can immediately revoke access to all encrypted data by disabling the KMS key
No encryption to avoid key management complexity
Client-side encryption only without any server-side encryption

explanations.a: SSE-S3 with AWS-managed keys gives AWS control over key rotation — the organization cannot control rotation schedules or immediately revoke access.
explanations.b: Customer-managed KMS keys give the organization complete control: configurable rotation schedules, full key usage audit trail, and immediate data access revocation by disabling the key — directly meeting the regulatory requirement. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: No encryption fails the regulatory requirement for data protection.
explanations.d: Client-side encryption alone does not provide server-side protection and does not give server-level access control needed for immediate revocation.

questions.text: What cost requirements should be evaluated before provisioning cloud resources?

answers.text: The color scheme of the cloud cost management dashboard

Budget constraints, expected usage patterns, billing model optimization (reserved vs. on-demand vs. spot), data transfer costs, and the total cost of ownership including storage, compute, and egress fees
The number of cloud providers the team has previously used
The age of the engineers provisioning the resources

explanations.a: Dashboard aesthetics are irrelevant to cost planning.
explanations.b: Cost requirements for provisioning include: budget limits that constrain resource sizing, usage pattern analysis to select the optimal billing model (reserved for stable, on-demand for variable, spot for fault-tolerant), data transfer cost estimation, and complete TCO analysis covering all cloud cost dimensions. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Provider history is a team experience consideration — not a cost provisioning requirement.
explanations.d: Engineer age is irrelevant to cloud cost planning.

questions.text: A team is given a budget of $5,000/month to provision a new cloud environment. Their initial configuration estimate is $8,000/month. Which cost optimization levers should they evaluate to meet the budget?

answers.text: Abandon the project since the budget cannot be met

Right-size over-provisioned instances to match actual requirements, switch stable workloads to reserved instances for discounts, use spot instances for fault-tolerant batch jobs, select lower-cost storage tiers for infrequently accessed data, and eliminate unused resource allocations
Provision all resources and exceed the budget — request additional budget after the fact
Use only the cheapest instance types regardless of performance requirements

explanations.a: A 38% budget gap is addressable through optimization — cloud cost optimization routinely achieves 30-60% reductions.
explanations.b: Rightsizing, reserved pricing, spot instances for appropriate workloads, storage tiering, and eliminating idle resources are the standard cloud cost optimization levers that collectively can reduce $8,000 estimates to within a $5,000 budget. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Exceeding budget without approval creates financial governance violations — optimization before provisioning is the correct approach.
explanations.d: Minimum-cost instances without performance validation risks under-provisioning that causes application failures — cost optimization must respect performance requirements.

questions.text: A team provisions cloud resources and implements a cost allocation strategy using tags. After one month, the finance team reports that 25% of cloud costs have no tag and cannot be attributed to any project or team. Which provisioning governance control MOST directly prevents this?

answers.text: Send monthly email reminders asking engineers to tag their resources

Implement cloud policy enforcement that prevents resource provisioning unless required cost allocation tags (project, team, environment) are provided at creation time — making tagging a mandatory provisioning prerequisite
Review untagged resources quarterly and add tags manually
Accept that 25% of costs will always be unattributed in cloud environments

explanations.a: Email reminders are voluntary — they do not technically prevent untagged resource creation.
explanations.b: Policy enforcement at provisioning time (AWS Service Control Policies, Azure Policy) that blocks resource creation without required tags eliminates the problem at the source — resources cannot exist without cost allocation tags, guaranteeing 100% attribution. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Quarterly manual tagging is reactive — it corrects existing untagged resources but does not prevent new ones from being created untagged.
explanations.d: 25% unattributed cost is a governance failure — not an accepted norm in well-managed cloud environments.

questions.text: An architect provisions a 3-year reserved instance for a workload. 18 months later, the workload is redesigned and no longer needs that instance type. What cost risk does this illustrate about provisioning decisions?

answers.text: Reserved instances automatically convert to the new instance type needed at no additional cost

Long-term commitment decisions made at provisioning time carry financial risk if workload requirements change — the team continues paying for the committed reserved instance even if the workload changes, unless they can sell the reservation on the marketplace or convert it
Cloud providers refund the unused portion of reserved instances when workloads change
Reserved instances automatically terminate when workloads no longer need them

explanations.a: Reserved instance convertibility varies by type — not all allow free conversion to different instance types.
explanations.b: Committing to a 3-year reservation assumes workload stability. When requirements change, the organization is locked into the committed pricing for the remainder of the term — paying for capacity it no longer needs. This illustrates the importance of matching commitment length to workload stability confidence. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Cloud providers do not refund unused reserved instance time — the commitment is a financial obligation.
explanations.d: Reserved instances are billing commitments — they do not automatically terminate when workloads change.

questions.text: What availability requirements must be considered when provisioning cloud resources?

answers.text: The cloud provider's stock market performance
Required uptime SLAs, acceptable downtime windows, RTO and RPO targets, geographic redundancy needs, and whether the workload requires multi-AZ or multi-region deployment
The number of engineers available to respond to incidents
The provider's data center construction materials

explanations.a: Provider stock market performance is a financial market metric — not a cloud resource availability requirement.
explanations.b: Availability requirements define how the infrastructure must be architected: uptime SLAs determine redundancy levels, RTO/RPO targets drive backup and replication strategies, geographic requirements determine whether multi-AZ or multi-region deployment is needed — all shaping provisioning decisions. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Incident response staffing is an operational concern — not an infrastructure provisioning requirement.
explanations.d: Data center construction materials are the provider's infrastructure concern — not a customer provisioning requirement.

questions.text: A company requires 99.95% availability for their customer portal. The cloud provider's single-instance SLA is 99.5%. Which provisioning approach achieves the 99.95% target?

answers.text: Deploy a single large instance and rely on the provider's 99.5% SLA

Deploy multiple instances across at least two availability zones with an Application Load Balancer — achieving higher combined availability than a single instance SLA by eliminating single AZ as a single point of failure
Purchase a premium support plan from the cloud provider to increase the SLA
Accept the 99.5% SLA as close enough to the 99.95% requirement

explanations.a: A single instance with 99.5% SLA does not meet the 99.95% target — single instances are not designed for high-availability deployments.
explanations.b: Multi-AZ deployment with a load balancer distributes availability risk across independent failure domains — two instances each with 99.5% availability achieve approximately 99.9975% combined availability, exceeding the 99.95% requirement. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Support plans improve response time during incidents — they do not change infrastructure availability SLAs.
explanations.d: 99.5% vs 99.95% represents a 3x difference in allowable downtime — accepting a miss of this magnitude violates the stated business requirement.

questions.text: An architect provisions cloud resources for a system with an RTO of 5 minutes and RPO of 1 minute. Which combination of provisioning decisions BEST satisfies both requirements?

answers.text: Daily backups to cold storage and manual failover procedures

Active-active multi-AZ deployment with synchronous database replication, automated health checks, and automated traffic failover — enabling near-zero RTO through automatic instance replacement and near-zero RPO through synchronous data replication
Weekly backups with a warm standby site and 2-hour manual failover process
Spot instances with periodic snapshots to cold storage

explanations.a: Daily backups result in up to 24 hours of data loss (violating 1-minute RPO) and manual failover cannot achieve 5-minute RTO consistently.
explanations.b: Active-active multi-AZ with synchronous replication ensures zero data loss (satisfying 1-minute RPO with essentially 0-second actual RPO) and automated failover completes in seconds to minutes (satisfying the 5-minute RTO). This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Weekly backups allow 7 days of data loss (catastrophically violates 1-minute RPO) and 2-hour manual failover violates 5-minute RTO.
explanations.d: Spot instances can be interrupted at any time and cold storage snapshots have retrieval delays — both violate the strict RTO/RPO requirements.

questions.text: A provisioning team debates whether to deploy a non-critical internal reporting tool with multi-AZ redundancy (adding 40% to cost) or a single AZ (cheaper but with potential downtime). Which decision framework BEST guides this choice?

answers.text: Always deploy multi-AZ regardless of criticality to maximize availability

Evaluate the business impact of downtime for the reporting tool — if scheduled monthly reports can tolerate hours of downtime at minimal business cost, single AZ may be appropriate; if real-time reporting drives business decisions, multi-AZ justifies the 40% premium
Always deploy single AZ to minimize cost regardless of criticality
Availability requirements are identical for all applications — criticality does not factor into provisioning decisions

explanations.a: Universal multi-AZ for all applications regardless of criticality wastes money on availability levels beyond business requirements.
explanations.b: Availability investment should match business criticality — a non-critical monthly reporting tool with low impact downtime may not justify a 40% premium for multi-AZ redundancy. A reporting tool driving real-time business decisions may require it. Business impact analysis drives the right decision. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Cost minimization as the sole criterion ignores availability requirements for business-critical applications.
explanations.d: Applications have varying criticality — availability requirements and provisioning decisions should reflect the business impact of each application's downtime.

questions.text: Why must compliance requirements be addressed during cloud resource provisioning rather than after deployment?

answers.text: Compliance requirements are only relevant during annual audits — not provisioning

Provisioning resources without compliance controls creates non-compliant infrastructure that may require costly remediation, cause audit failures, or result in regulatory fines — compliance must be built in from the start, not retrofitted
Cloud providers automatically ensure compliance for all provisioned resources
Compliance requirements only apply to on-premises infrastructure

explanations.a: Compliance is an ongoing operational requirement — not an annual-only concern.
explanations.b: Security and compliance built into provisioning (encryption, access controls, logging) is far simpler and less costly than retrofitting controls onto running production systems. Non-compliant resources may expose data, fail audits, and incur regulatory penalties before remediation is complete. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Cloud providers offer compliant infrastructure services but customers must configure their resources compliantly — compliance is a shared responsibility.
explanations.d: Compliance regulations apply to cloud deployments — regulatory bodies do not exempt cloud-hosted systems.

questions.text: A healthcare company provisions cloud resources that will store Protected Health Information (PHI). Which compliance requirements MUST be implemented at provisioning time under HIPAA?

answers.text: No special requirements — standard cloud security is sufficient for PHI

Encryption of PHI at rest and in transit, access controls limiting PHI access to authorized users only, comprehensive audit logging of all PHI access, a signed Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with the cloud provider, and deployment in HIPAA-eligible cloud services
PHI can be stored in any cloud service as long as a password is required for access
HIPAA only requires patient consent for cloud storage — no technical controls are mandated

explanations.a: Standard cloud security does not automatically meet HIPAA requirements — specific technical safeguards are mandated.
explanations.b: HIPAA mandates: encryption for PHI at rest and in transit, minimum necessary access controls, audit controls tracking who accessed PHI when, and a BAA with cloud providers acting as business associates. Only HIPAA-eligible cloud services with a signed BAA can lawfully store PHI. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Password-only protection is insufficient — HIPAA requires encryption, access controls, and audit logging as technical safeguards.
explanations.d: HIPAA has extensive technical safeguard requirements beyond patient consent — this statement severely understates HIPAA obligations.

questions.text: A cloud architect must provision resources that satisfy both PCI DSS (payment data) and SOC2 (service organization controls) compliance. Which provisioning approach BEST addresses dual compliance frameworks simultaneously?

answers.text: Choose one framework and ignore the other to simplify provisioning

Identify the union of controls required by both frameworks — provisions that satisfy the more stringent requirements of each framework simultaneously, implement compensating controls where frameworks conflict, and document the compliance mapping for both auditors
Obtain PCI DSS certification first, then SOC2 in a separate project phase
Dual compliance is impossible in cloud environments

explanations.a: Ignoring one compliance framework creates regulatory violations and audit failures — dual compliance must address all applicable requirements.
explanations.b: Many PCI DSS and SOC2 controls overlap (encryption, access control, logging, incident response). Provisioning to satisfy the union of requirements from both frameworks — implementing the more stringent standard when they conflict — achieves dual compliance efficiently without duplicating effort. Documentation mapping controls to each framework satisfies auditors. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Sequential certification requires maintaining non-compliant posture for the second framework during the gap period — concurrent provisioning for both is preferable.
explanations.d: Major cloud providers are certified for both PCI DSS and SOC2 — dual compliance in cloud is absolutely achievable.

questions.text: A team provisions cloud resources and uses default configurations for all services. A compliance audit finds multiple violations including unencrypted storage, overly permissive security groups, and missing audit logs. What provisioning practice MOST effectively prevents this outcome?

answers.text: Run compliance checks only after the audit report is received

Implement compliance-as-code using tools like AWS Config Rules, Azure Policy, or OPA/Sentinel that automatically evaluate every provisioned resource against compliance policies — blocking or alerting on non-compliant configurations at provisioning time
Trust that cloud provider default configurations are compliance-ready
Perform a manual compliance review annually after deployment

explanations.a: Post-audit remediation is reactive — resources operate non-compliantly until the audit finding triggers fixes.
explanations.b: Compliance-as-code automatically evaluates provisioned resources against policies in real time — blocking non-compliant configurations (preventive) or immediately alerting for remediation (detective). This prevents compliance violations from reaching production rather than discovering them during audits. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Cloud provider defaults are designed for broad usability — they are typically not compliance-ready for regulated industries without additional configuration.
explanations.d: Annual compliance reviews leave up to 12 months of non-compliant exposure — continuous automated evaluation is required.

questions.text: Which network requirements must be defined before provisioning cloud infrastructure?

answers.text: The physical cable type used in the cloud provider's data center
IP address ranges (CIDR blocks), subnet design, routing requirements, connectivity needs (VPN/Direct Connect), firewall rules, load balancing requirements, and expected network throughput
The number of network engineers on the team
The color of network status indicator LEDs on the cloud hardware

explanations.a: Physical cable types are the provider's infrastructure — not customer network provisioning requirements.
explanations.b: Network provisioning requires defining: VPC CIDR to avoid overlap with on-premises, subnet design for tier isolation, routing for internet and private connectivity, hybrid connectivity method, security groups and ACL rules, load balancer requirements for distributed traffic, and bandwidth needs for instance type selection. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Team size is a staffing concern — not a network provisioning requirement.
explanations.d: Hardware LED colors are physical infrastructure aesthetics — not network requirements.

questions.text: A team provisions a cloud VPC without planning IP addressing. Later, they try to establish VPC peering with the on-premises network but discover both use 10.0.0.0/8. Which network requirement planning step would have prevented this?

answers.text: Using only IPv6 addresses to avoid IPv4 overlap issues

Defining the VPC CIDR block upfront as part of network requirements — selecting a non-overlapping range that avoids conflicts with all existing networks (on-premises, other VPCs, partner networks) the environment will eventually need to connect to
Relying on NAT to resolve any future IP address conflicts
IP address planning is only necessary for on-premises networks — cloud VPCs can use any range

explanations.a: IPv6 adoption does not eliminate the need for IPv4 planning — hybrid connectivity often requires both.
explanations.b: Network IP planning at provisioning time should map all existing and anticipated future networks to identify and avoid CIDR conflicts. A non-overlapping VPC CIDR must be selected before provisioning to enable future peering — changing VPC CIDR after provisioning requires recreation. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: NAT can work around some overlap scenarios but requires complex double-NAT configurations and does not enable VPC peering (which requires non-overlapping CIDRs).
explanations.d: VPC CIDR planning is critical — overlapping CIDRs prevent VPC peering and create hybrid connectivity problems exactly as described.

questions.text: A team provisions cloud resources for an application that must meet strict latency requirements between its three microservices. The services are deployed in different availability zones for redundancy. Post-deployment monitoring shows inter-AZ network latency adds 2-5ms per call. With 20 synchronous service calls per request, this adds 40-100ms total latency. How should network requirements have been considered at provisioning time?

answers.text: Deploy all microservices in the same AZ to minimize latency at the cost of redundancy

At provisioning time, model the expected latency budget accounting for inter-AZ call overhead — either accepting the latency trade-off of multi-AZ for redundancy, or redesigning the service interaction pattern to reduce synchronous call chains (e.g., async messaging, batching), or co-locating latency-sensitive service pairs within the same AZ
Increase instance sizes to reduce the per-call latency from inter-AZ communication
Network latency between AZs is irrelevant to application design decisions

explanations.a: Single-AZ deployment eliminates redundancy — the multi-AZ availability requirement exists for a reason and cannot simply be discarded.
explanations.b: Network latency requirements must be modeled at provisioning time accounting for architectural decisions: multi-AZ inter-service calls add predictable latency that must fit within the total latency budget. If they do not, the service interaction pattern needs redesign (async, batching, local caching) before provisioning — not discovered post-deployment. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Instance size affects compute throughput — it does not reduce physical network propagation delay between AZs.
explanations.d: Inter-AZ network latency is a real and predictable constraint that directly impacts application architecture — ignoring it causes exactly the post-deployment discovery described.

questions.text: A company provisions cloud resources and later discovers their 10Gbps instance generates 8Gbps of inter-region data transfer for analytics — costing $7,200/month in egress fees. Which network requirement planning step would have prevented this cost surprise?

answers.text: Network egress costs are always negligible and do not need to be planned

Estimating expected inter-region data transfer volumes as part of network requirements — calculating egress costs at provisioning time and evaluating architectural alternatives (local analytics processing, data lake in the same region, compressed transfer) to minimize egress fees before provisioning
Cloud providers do not charge for inter-region data transfer
Egress fees only apply when transferring data to on-premises — not inter-region

explanations.a: Egress fees are frequently the largest unexpected cloud cost item — they absolutely require pre-provisioning planning.
explanations.b: Network requirements must include data transfer volume estimates and egress cost calculations. At 8Gbps continuous transfer, the $7,200/month could have been avoided by co-locating analytics with data sources, compressing data before transfer, or using a provider with lower inter-region egress pricing — all decisions that require cost analysis before provisioning architecture. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Cloud providers charge data egress fees for inter-region transfers — this statement is incorrect.
explanations.d: Inter-region data transfer within the same cloud provider incurs egress fees — not only transfers to on-premises.

questions.text: Which compute requirements must be assessed before provisioning cloud instances?

answers.text: The team's favorite programming language and development environment

CPU type and count requirements, memory (RAM) needs, GPU requirements for specialized workloads, operating system compatibility, expected concurrent workload volume, and whether the workload is compute-intensive, memory-intensive, or general-purpose
The number of coffee machines in the office where engineers work
The cloud provider's employee count

explanations.a: Developer tooling preferences are not cloud provisioning requirements.
explanations.b: Compute requirements drive instance family selection: compute-optimized for CPU-intensive workloads, memory-optimized for in-memory databases, GPU instances for ML/graphics, ARM vs x86 for OS compatibility, and instance sizing based on concurrent workload volume — all must be assessed before provisioning. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Office amenities are irrelevant to cloud compute provisioning requirements.
explanations.d: Provider employee count has no bearing on compute provisioning decisions.

questions.text: A team provisions general-purpose instances for an in-memory analytics workload that processes 500GB datasets entirely in RAM. Six hours after deployment, the instances experience heavy swap usage and severe performance degradation. What compute requirement was missed during provisioning?

answers.text: The instances needed faster CPUs to process the dataset

The memory requirement was not properly assessed — an in-memory analytics workload processing 500GB datasets requires instances with sufficient RAM to hold the entire working set. Memory-optimized instances (e.g., R-series) with 512GB+ RAM should have been selected
The instances needed more vCPUs to handle the 500GB dataset
The storage IOPS were insufficient for the analytics workload

explanations.a: CPU performance is secondary to memory capacity for an in-memory workload — swap usage indicates memory exhaustion, not CPU bottleneck.
explanations.b: In-memory analytics requires the entire dataset to fit in RAM. General-purpose instances typically have 4-8GB RAM per vCPU — far below the 500GB requirement. Memory-optimized instances with 500GB+ RAM should have been selected based on the in-memory processing requirement. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: vCPU count addresses processing parallelism — the swap usage clearly indicates memory capacity, not CPU, is the constraint.
explanations.d: Storage IOPS affect disk-based workloads — an in-memory workload that has exhausted RAM is swapping to disk, but the root cause is insufficient RAM, not insufficient IOPS.

questions.text: A team must provision compute for a workload that has two distinct phases: a 2-hour data ingestion phase requiring 32 vCPUs and a 6-hour analytics phase requiring 128 vCPUs. Both phases run sequentially once per day. Which compute provisioning strategy MOST optimizes cost for this pattern?

answers.text: Provision 128 vCPU instances running 24/7 to handle both phases at any time

Use reserved instances for the baseline 32-vCPU ingestion phase and use on-demand or spot instances to add the additional 96 vCPUs only during the 6-hour analytics phase — paying for high compute only when it is actually needed
Provision 32 vCPU instances for all phases and accept slower analytics performance
Use a serverless function for the 128 vCPU analytics workload

explanations.a: Provisioning 128 vCPUs 24/7 pays for 96 vCPUs of unused capacity for 16 hours per day (during ingestion and idle time) — significant waste.
explanations.b: Reserved baseline for the stable ingestion workload provides cost savings for predictable usage. Burst compute added only during the analytics phase with on-demand or spot pricing pays for peak capacity only during the 6-hour daily window — optimizing cost for each phase's distinct requirements. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Under-provisioning the analytics phase creates performance degradation that may make the 6-hour window insufficient for workload completion.
explanations.d: 128 vCPU compute workloads running for 6 hours exceed serverless function execution limits and compute capabilities — not an appropriate platform.

questions.text: A company provisions ARM-based cloud instances (e.g., AWS Graviton) for cost savings. Post-deployment, one of their Java applications crashes on startup. What compute requirement was not properly validated?

answers.text: The number of instances was insufficient

Architecture compatibility — some native libraries, compiled binaries, or JVM versions may not have ARM builds. Applications running on x86 instances cannot directly run on ARM instances without recompilation or compatible ARM builds of all dependencies
The instance did not have enough storage for the Java application
ARM instances do not support Java applications under any circumstances

explanations.a: Instance count does not cause startup crashes — architecture incompatibility does.
explanations.b: ARM-based instances require ARM-compatible builds of all application components including native libraries, JVM, and any compiled extensions. Applications built for x86 cannot run on ARM without recompilation. Compute requirement validation must include architecture compatibility testing before provisioning ARM instances for production. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Storage availability does not cause Java startup crashes — architecture incompatibility causes native library loading failures.
explanations.d: ARM instances fully support Java — but the JVM and all native libraries must be ARM-compatible builds, which not all older or third-party libraries provide.
